In this paper, we refute this and show that the algorithm of Vaze and Sinha [2025] simultaneously achieves regret of $O(\sqrt{T})$ regret and CCV of $O(T^{1/3})$ when $d=2$.

AI.tldr: The study analyzes constrained online convex optimization, improving the performance trade-off between static regret and cumulative constraint violation (CCV). It shows that achieving CCV of O(T^(1/3)) alongside regret of O(√T) is possible in certain conditions for d=2.
AI.motivation: To address the widely held conjecture that cumulative constraint violation (CCV) of Ω(√T) is inevitable when achieving static regret of O(√T), and to enhance understanding of constrained online convex optimization.
AI.method: The paper revisits known algorithms and extends their analysis in a two-dimensional action space (d=2), showing that improved performance bounds of O(T^(1/3)) for CCV can be achieved without compromising regret of O(√T).
AI.result: The algorithm of Vaze and Sinha (2025) is demonstrated to simultaneously achieve a regret of O(√T) and a cumulative constraint violation (CCV) of O(T^(1/3)) for the specific case when the dimensionality of the space is d=2.
AI.conclusion: This work challenges existing beliefs regarding the trade-offs between regret and CCV, providing new insights and improved guarantees for the constrained online convex optimization problem.

title: Neural collapse in the orthoplex regime
summary: When training a neural network for classification, the feature vectors of the training set are known to collapse to the vertices of a regular simplex, provided the dimension $d$ of the feature space and the number $n$ of classes satisfies $n\leq d+1$. This phenomenon is known as neural collapse. For other applications like language models, one instead takes $n\gg d$. Here, the neural collapse phenomenon still occurs, but with different emergent geometric figures. We characterize these geometric figures in the orthoplex regime where $d+2\leq n\leq 2d$. The techniques in our analysis primarily involve Radon's theorem and convexity.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the geometric structures emerging during the neural collapse in the orthoplex regime when training neural networks.
AI.motivation: To explore the neural collapse phenomenon in scenarios where the number of classes far exceeds the feature space's dimension, particularly in the orthoplex regime.
AI.method: The study employs mathematical tools such as Radon's theorem and convexity to characterize the emergent geometric structures.
AI.result: The paper identifies and characterizes the geometric figures that appear in the orthoplex regime ($d+2 \leq n \leq 2d$).
AI.conclusion: The findings expand our understanding of neural collapse by showing how it manifests in high-class scenarios with $n \gg d$, enhancing insights for applications like language models.

title: When Negation Is a Geometry Problem in Vision-Language Models
comment: Accepted to CVPR (Multimodal Algorithmic Reasoning Workshop) 2026
summary: Joint Vision-Language Embedding models such as CLIP typically fail at understanding negation in text queries - for example, failing to distinguish "no" in the query: "a plain blue shirt with no logos". Prior work has largely addressed this limitation through data-centric approaches, fine-tuning CLIP on large-scale synthetic negation datasets. However, these efforts are commonly evaluated using retrieval-based metrics that cannot reliably reflect whether negation is actually understood. In this paper, we identify two key limitations of such evaluation metrics and investigate an alternative evaluation framework based on Multimodal LLMs-as-a-judge, which typically excel at understanding simple yes/no questions about image content, providing a fair evaluation of negation understanding in CLIP models. We then ask whether there already exists a direction in the CLIP embedding space associated with negation. We find evidence that such a direction exists, and show that it can be manipulated through test-time intervention via representation engineering to steer CLIP toward negation-aware behavior without any fine-tuning. Finally, we test negation understanding on non-common image-text samples to evaluate generalization under distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the challenge of joint vision-language embedding models, specifically CLIP, struggling with negation understanding in text queries. Traditional data-centric approaches have limitations, and the authors propose an alternative evaluation using Multimodal LLMs as judges while investigating negation directions in CLIP's embedding space.
AI.motivation: Joint vision-language models often fail to interpret negation in queries, leading to misinterpretations, which this paper aims to address for better model performance.
AI.method: The authors propose using Multimodal LLMs-as-a-judge for fair evaluation of negation understanding and explore representation engineering in CLIP's embedding space to manipulate and steer the model's behavior without fine-tuning.
AI.result: They find evidence of a negation-associated direction in CLIP's embedding space and demonstrate that test-time interventions can improve negation understanding.
AI.conclusion: Using representation adjustments, negation understanding in CLIP can be improved, providing a non-fine-tuning approach to enhance generalization under distribution shifts.

title: StreamSampling.jl: Efficient Sampling from Data Streams in Julia
comment: Submitted to the Proceedings of the JuliaCon Conferences
summary: StreamSampling$.$jl is a Julia library designed to provide general and efficient methods for sampling from data streams in a single pass, even when the total number of items is unknown. In this paper, we describe the capabilities of the library and its advantages over traditional sampling procedures, such as maintaining a small, constant memory footprint and avoiding the need to fully materialize the stream in memory. Furthermore, we provide empirical benchmarks comparing online sampling methods against standard approaches, demonstrating performance and memory improvements.

AI.tldr: StreamSampling$.$jl enables efficient single-pass data stream sampling with minimal memory use.
AI.motivation: The need to sample from data streams efficiently without fully materializing them in memory.
AI.method: Developing a Julia library (StreamSampling$.$jl) that maintains a constant memory footprint and implements online sampling techniques.
AI.result: Empirical benchmarks show improved performance and reduced memory usage compared to standard sampling methods.
AI.conclusion: StreamSampling$.$jl offers a practical solution for real-time data stream sampling with better efficiency and memory management.

title: RLVR Training of LLMs Does Not Improve Thinking Ability for General QA: Evaluation Method and a Simple Solution
summary: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) stimulates the thinking processes of large language models (LLMs), substantially enhancing their reasoning abilities on verifiable tasks. It is often assumed that similar gains should transfer to general question answering (GQA), but this assumption has not been thoroughly validated. To assess whether RLVR automatically improves LLM performance on GQA, we propose a Cross-Generation evaluation framework that measures the quality of intermediate reasoning by feeding the generated thinking context into LLMs of varying capabilities. Our evaluation leads to a discouraging finding: the efficacy of the thinking process on GQA tasks is markedly lower than on verifiable tasks, suggesting that explicit training on GQA remains necessary in addition to training on verifiable tasks. We further observe that direct RL training on GQA is less effective than RLVR. Our hypothesis is that, whereas verifiable tasks demand robust logical chains to obtain high rewards, GQA tasks often admit shortcuts to high rewards without cultivating high-quality thinking. To avoid possible shortcuts, we introduce a simple method, Separated Thinking And Response Training (START), which first trains only the thinking process, using rewards defined on the final answer. We show that START improves both the quality of thinking and the final answer across several GQA benchmarks and RL algorithms.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates the effect of Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) on general question answering (GQA) and finds it insufficient for robust transfer. They develop a new method called START to enhance reasoning processes in GQA tasks.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to assess whether the reasoning abilities gained by RLVR in verifiable tasks can transfer automatically to GQA tasks, as this assumption hasn't been rigorously validated.
AI.method: The authors propose a Cross-Generation evaluation framework to measure reasoning quality on GQA by using LLMs of varying capabilities and design a method called START that separates training for thinking processes and final answers.
AI.result: Their findings indicate that RLVR does not effectively transfer to GQA tasks, as GQA allows shortcuts to high rewards. START proves to improve reasoning and answer accuracy across GQA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicit GQA-specific training is still necessary, and START offers a promising way to enhance thinking quality and results in GQA.

title: Architecture for Multi-Unmanned Aerial Vehicles based Autonomous Precision Agriculture Systems
summary: The use of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) in precision agriculture has seen a huge increase recently. As such, systems that aim to apply various algorithms on the field need a structured framework of abstractions. This paper defines the various tasks of the UAVs in precision agriculture and model them into an architectural framework. The presented architecture is built on the context that there will be minimal physical intervention to do the tasks defined with multiple coordinated and cooperative UAVs. Various tasks such as image processing, path planning, communication, data acquisition, and field mapping are employed in the architecture to provide an efficient system. Besides, different limitation for applying Multi-UAVs in precision agriculture has been considered in designing the architecture. The architecture provides an autonomous end-to-end solution, starting from mission planning, data acquisition and image processing framework that is highly efficient and can enable farmers to comprehensively deploy UAVs onto their lands. Simulation and field tests shows that the architecture offers a number of advantages that include fault-tolerance, robustness, developer and user-friendliness.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an architectural framework for using multi-UAVs in precision agriculture, focusing on minimal physical intervention and efficient task execution.
AI.motivation: The increasing use of UAVs in precision agriculture necessitates a structured framework to ensure efficient implementation of various algorithms on the field.
AI.method: The paper defines tasks such as image processing, path planning, data acquisition, and field mapping, and integrates them into an architectural framework tailored for multi-UAV systems.
AI.result: Simulation and field tests demonstrate that the architecture is fault-tolerant, robust, user-friendly, and efficient for precision agricultural tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed end-to-end architecture facilitates autonomous mission planning, data processing, and comprehensive UAV deployment, making it highly advantageous for farmers.

title: Achieving $\widetilde{O}(1/ε)$ Sample Complexity for Bilinear Systems Identification under Bounded Noises
summary: This paper studies finite-sample set-membership identification for discrete-time bilinear systems under bounded symmetric log-concave disturbances. Compared with existing finite-sample results for linear systems and related analyses under stronger noise assumptions, we consider the more challenging bilinear setting with trajectory-dependent regressors and allow marginally stable dynamics with polynomial mean-square state growth. Under these conditions, we prove that the diameter of the feasible parameter set shrinks with sample complexity $\widetilde{O}(1/ε)$. Simulation supports the theory and illustrates the advantage of the proposed estimator for uncertainty quantification.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses set-membership system identification for bilinear systems under specific disturbance conditions, proving feasible parameter set diameter reduces as samples increase.
AI.motivation: There is a need to handle identification challenges in bilinear systems with trajectory-dependent regressors and marginally stable dynamics, which are more complex than linear systems.
AI.method: The authors examine finite-sample set-membership identification under symmetric log-concave disturbances, deriving theoretical bounds and validating outcomes through simulations.
AI.result: The study shows the diameter of the feasible parameter set decreases with a sample complexity of $\widetilde{O}(1/ε)$, supported by simulation outcomes highlighting the proposed estimator's advantages.
AI.conclusion: The research validates that the proposed methodology effectively enables parameter estimation in bilinear systems, providing improved uncertainty quantification under complex dynamics.

title: The Intelligent Disobedience Game: Formulating Disobedience in Stackelberg Games and Markov Decision Processes
comment: Accepted for presentation at the Rebellion and Disobedience in AI (RaD-AI) at AAMAS 2026
summary: In shared autonomy, a critical tension arises when an automated assistant must choose between obeying a human's instruction and deliberately overriding it to prevent harm. This safety-critical behavior is known as intelligent disobedience. To formalize this dynamic, this paper introduces the Intelligent Disobedience Game (IDG), a sequential game-theoretic framework based on Stackelberg games that models the interaction between a human leader and an assistive follower operating under asymmetric information. It characterizes optimal strategies for both agents across multi-step scenarios, identifying strategic phenomena such as ``safety traps,'' where the system indefinitely avoids harm but fails to achieve the human's goal. The IDG provides a needed mathematical foundation that enables both the algorithmic development of agents that can learn safe non-compliance and the empirical study of how humans perceive and trust disobedient AI. The paper further translates the IDG into a shared control Multi-Agent Markov Decision Process representation, forming a compact computational testbed for training reinforcement learning agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Intelligent Disobedience Game (IDG), a framework for modeling the balance between human instructions and AI's safety-driven decisions, offering a mathematical and computational basis for safe non-compliance.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge in shared autonomy where automated systems must balance following human instructions with overriding them to ensure safety, necessitating a formal framework to study and develop such behavior.
AI.method: The authors propose the Intelligent Disobedience Game (IDG), a sequential game-theoretic model based on Stackelberg games, and convert it into a compact Multi-Agent Markov Decision Process for computational exploration.
AI.result: The paper establishes optimal strategies for humans and AI in multi-step scenarios, identifies safety traps, and offers a testbed for reinforcement learning to enable safe non-compliance.
AI.conclusion: The developed framework provides a formal and empirical foundation for designing AI systems capable of intelligent disobedience, enabling advancements in shared autonomy and human-AI interaction trust.

title: Generating from Discrete Distributions Using Diffusions: Insights from Random Constraint Satisfaction Problems
summary: Generating data from discrete distributions is important for a number of application domains including text, tabular data, and genomic data. Several groups have recently used random $k$-satisfiability ($k$-SAT) as a synthetic benchmark for new generative techniques. In this paper, we show that fundamental insights from the theory of random constraint satisfaction problems have observable implications (sometime contradicting intuition) on the behavior of generative techniques on such benchmarks. More precisely, we study the problem of generating a uniformly random solution of a given (random) $k$-SAT or $k$-XORSAT formula. Among other findings, we observe that: $(i)$~Continuous diffusions outperform masked discrete diffusions; $(ii)$~Learned diffusions can match the theoretical `ideal' accuracy; $(iii)$~Smart ordering of the variables can significantly improve accuracy, although not following popular heuristics.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the performance of generative techniques on $k$-SAT and $k$-XORSAT problems, offering surprising observations tied to theoretical insights from random constraint satisfaction problems.
AI.motivation: The paper motivates the need for efficient and accurate methods to generate data from discrete distributions, particularly given the importance of these tasks in domains like text, tabular, and genomic data analysis.
AI.method: The authors study the problem of generating a uniformly random solution for a given random $k$-SAT or $k$-XORSAT formula, comparing different generative approaches and analyzing their performance with theoretical insights.
AI.result: The findings include: (1) Continuous diffusions outperform masked discrete diffusions, (2) Learned diffusions can achieve ideal theoretical accuracy, and (3) Optimizing the variable ordering significantly improves accuracy, defying conventional heuristics.
AI.conclusion: The analysis highlights the relevance of theoretical insights in improving generative modeling benchmarks for discrete problems and identifies techniques that outperform popular methods.

title: Improving Diffusion Generalization with Weak-to-Strong Segmented Guidance
summary: Diffusion models generate synthetic images through an iterative refinement process. However, the misalignment between the simulation-free objective and the iterative process often causes accumulated gradient error along the sampling trajectory, which leads to unsatisfactory results and a failure to generalize. Guidance techniques like Classifier Free Guidance (CFG) and AutoGuidance (AG) alleviate this by extrapolating between the main and inferior signal for stronger generalization. Despite empirical success, the effective operational regimes of prevalent guidance methods are still under-explored, leading to ambiguity when selecting the appropriate guidance method given a precondition. In this work, we first conduct synthetic comparisons to isolate and demonstrate the effective regime of guidance methods represented by CFG and AG from the perspective of weak-to-strong principle. Based on this, we propose a hybrid instantiation called SGG under the principle, taking the benefits of both. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the W2S principle along with SGG can be migrated into the training objective, improving the generalization ability of unguided diffusion models. We validate our approach with comprehensive experiments. At inference time, evaluations on SD3 and SD3.5 confirm that SGG outperforms existing training-free guidance variants. Training-time experiments on transformer architectures demonstrate the effective migration and performance gains in both conditional and unconditional settings. Code is available at https://github.com/851695e35/SGG.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SGG as a hybrid guidance method for diffusion models, addressing gradient error accumulation and improving generalization. Experiments confirm its effectiveness both in inference and training-time scenarios.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to address the issue of accumulated gradient error and a lack of generalization in existing diffusion models caused by misaligned objectives, along with the under-explored operational regimes of guidance methods.
AI.method: The authors conduct synthetic comparisons of prevailing guidance methods (CFG, AG) based on a weak-to-strong principle, propose SGG as a hybrid solution, and transfer the principle into training objectives for unguided models.
AI.result: SGG outperforms other guidance methods in inference-time experiments on SD3 and SD3.5. Additionally, training-time experiments showcase improved generalization and performance on transformer architectures.
AI.conclusion: SGG, as a hybrid guidance technique built on the weak-to-strong principle, demonstrates improved generalization abilities and operational effectiveness, enhancing diffusion models through both training and inference processes.

title: Dynamic analysis enhances issue resolution
summary:
Translating natural language descriptions into viable code fixes remains a fundamental challenge in software engineering. While the proliferation of agentic large language models (LLMs) has vastly improved automated repository-level debugging, current frameworks hit a ceiling when dealing with sophisticated bugs like implicit type degradations and complex polymorphic control flows. Because these methods rely heavily on static analysis and superficial execution feedback, they lack visibility into intermediate runtime states. Consequently, agents are forced into costly, speculative trial-and-error loops, wasting computational tokens without successfully isolating the root cause.
  To bridge this gap, we propose DAIRA (Dynamic Analysis-enhanced Issue Resolution Agent), a pioneering automated repair framework that natively embeds dynamic analysis into the agent's reasoning cycle. Driven by a Test Tracing-Driven methodology, DAIRA utilizes lightweight monitors to extract critical runtime data -- such as variable mutations and call stacks -- and synthesizes them into structured semantic reports. This mechanism fundamentally shifts the agent's behavior from blind guesswork to evidence-based, deterministic deduction. When powered by Gemini 3 Flash Preview, DAIRA establishes a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, achieving a 79.4% resolution rate on the SWE-bench Verified dataset. Compared to existing baselines, our framework not only conquers highly complex defects but also cuts overall inference expenses by roughly 10% and decreases input token consumption by approximately 25%.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DAIRA, a framework that integrates dynamic analysis to improve automated bug fixing by large language models, surpassing current limitations in handling complex bugs.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing debugging tools that fail to deal effectively with complex bugs, including implicit type degradations and polymorphic control flows, due to their reliance on static analysis and trial-and-error approaches.
AI.method: The authors propose DAIRA, a framework based on a Test Tracing-Driven methodology, which uses lightweight monitors to gather runtime data such as variable mutations and call stacks. This data is synthesized into structured semantic reports to enable evidence-based debugging by LLMs.
AI.result: DAIRA achieves a 79.4% resolution rate on the SWE-bench Verified dataset, surpasses existing baselines in handling complex bugs, reduces inference costs by 10%, and cuts token consumption by 25%.
AI.conclusion: DAIRA represents a significant advancement in automated debugging frameworks by leveraging dynamic analysis to handle complex software bugs more effectively and efficiently, setting a new standard in the domain.

title: BenchBench: Benchmarking Automated Benchmark Generation
summary: Benchmarks are the de facto standard for tracking progress in large language models (LLMs), yet static test sets can rapidly saturate, become vulnerable to contamination, and are costly to refresh. Scalable evaluation of open-ended items often relies on LLM judges, introducing additional sources of bias and prompt sensitivity. We argue that evaluation must extend beyond how well models answer benchmarks to how well models design them. We introduce BenchBench, a three-stage pipeline and dataset for benchmarking automated benchmark generation: (i) extract structured domain cards from seed benchmarks, (ii) prompt multiple designer LLMs to generate quota-controlled suites, and (iii) validate items with a multi-model answerer panel using exact/numeric/symbolic verifiers when possible and rubric-guided judging otherwise, yielding designer--answerer matrices with item-level quality flags and psychometric diagnostics. Across nine variants spanning computer science, mathematics, medicine, and theory-of-mind reasoning (including multilingual and multimodal settings), we generate 16.7K items, retain ~15K core items post-filtering, and produce ~152K graded model--item responses. BenchBench shows that benchmark-design ability is only moderately correlated with answer-time strength (Spearman rho ~0.37), invalidity is negatively associated with discrimination (Pearson r~0.62), and the resulting designer--answerer matrices enable scalable audits of format/modality/language fidelity and suite-dependent self/family interactions. The project is available at: https://github.com/koanatakiyo/BenchBench.

AI.tldr: BenchBench introduces a novel evaluation framework focusing on how well large language models (LLMs) can design benchmarks, aiming to improve dynamism and reduce biases in scalable evaluations.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks for LLMs often saturate quickly, risk contamination, and are expensive to update. Moreover, evaluations relying on LLM judgments introduce biases and sensitivity issues.
AI.method: BenchBench uses a three-stage pipeline: (1) extract structured domain cards, (2) generate benchmark suites via LLM designers under defined quotas, (3) validate generated items using multi-model answerer panels and psychometric diagnostics.
AI.result: The study generated 16.7K items, retained ~15K core items after filtering, and graded ~152K model responses. The findings show moderate correlation between benchmark-design ability and answer-time strength, and highlight usability for scalable audits.
AI.conclusion: BenchBench demonstrates that LLMs' ability to design benchmarks opens possibilities for scalable, dynamic, and bias-conscious evaluations. It provides actionable insights into item validity, discrimination, and model-family interactions.

title: GAPG: Geometry Aware Push-Grasping Synergy for Goal-Oriented Manipulation in Clutter
summary: Grasping target objects is a fundamental skill for robotic manipulation, but in cluttered environments with stacked or occluded objects, a single-step grasp is often insufficient. To address this, previous work has introduced pushing as an auxiliary action to create graspable space. However, these methods often struggle with both stability and efficiency because they neglect the scene's geometric information, which is essential for evaluating grasp robustness and ensuring that pushing actions are safe and effective. To this end, we propose a geometry-aware push-grasp synergy framework that leverages point cloud data to integrate grasp and push evaluation. Specifically, the grasp evaluation module analyzes the geometric relationship between the gripper's point cloud and the points enclosed within its closing region to determine grasp feasibility and stability. Guided by this, the push evaluation module predicts how pushing actions influence future graspable space, enabling the robot to select actions that reliably transform non-graspable states into graspable ones. By jointly reasoning about geometry in both grasping and pushing, our framework achieves safer, more efficient, and more reliable manipulation in cluttered settings. Our method is extensively tested in simulation and real-world environments in various scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that our model generalizes well to real-world scenes and unseen objects.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a geometry-aware push-grasp synergy framework using point cloud data to enhance robotic manipulation in cluttered settings with stacked or occluded objects. It integrates grasp and push evaluation for safer and more efficient task execution.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for robotic manipulation in cluttered environments struggle with stability and efficiency due to neglecting geometric information vital for safe and effective pushing and grasping actions.
AI.method: The framework uses point cloud data to analyze geometric relationships for grasp feasibility and stability, while a push evaluation module predicts how pushing impacts future graspable spaces. These two components jointly reason about geometry for better action planning.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates improved safety, efficiency, and reliability in robotic manipulation tasks, validated through extensive simulations and real-world experiments across various challenging scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method advances robotic manipulation by effectively integrating geometric reasoning into push and grasp evaluation, yielding robust performance even with unseen objects and real-world complexities.

title: Unfolding with a Wasserstein Loss
summary:
Data unfolding -- the removal of noise or artifacts from measurements -- is a fundamental task across the experimental sciences. Of particular interest are applications in physics, where the dominant approach is Richardson-Lucy (RL) deconvolution. The classical RL approach aims to find denoised data that, once passed through the noise model, is as close as possible to the measured data in terms of Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence. This requires that the support of the measured data overlaps with the output of the noise model, a hypothesis typically enforced by binning, which introduces numerical error.
  As a counterpoint, the present work studies an alternative formulation using a Wasserstein loss. We establish sharp conditions for existence and uniqueness of optimizers, answering open questions of Li, et al., regarding necessary conditions for existence and uniqueness in the case of transport map noise models. We then develop a provably convergent generalized Sinkhorn algorithm to compute approximate optimizers. Our algorithm requires only empirical observations of the noise model and measured data and scales with the size of the data, rather than the ambient dimension. Numerical experiments on one- and two-dimensional problems inspired by jet mass unfolding in particle physics demonstrate that the optimal transport approach offers robust, accurate performance compared to classical RL deconvolution, particularly when binning artifacts are significant.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an alternative to Richardson-Lucy (RL) deconvolution, using a Wasserstein loss for data unfolding, aiming to reduce binning artifacts and improve performance.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address limitations in Richardson-Lucy (RL) deconvolution, particularly the numerical errors introduced by binning in the classical approach.
AI.method: The paper formulates a new unfolding approach using a Wasserstein loss. It establishes conditions for existence and uniqueness of solutions, answers theoretical questions about transport map noise models, and develops a generalized Sinkhorn algorithm for computation.
AI.result: The approach demonstrates robust and accurate performance in both one- and two-dimensional numerical experiments, addressing challenges where binning artifacts are significant.
AI.conclusion: This alternative framework using Wasserstein loss offers advantages over classical RL deconvolution, with fewer artifacts and strong theoretical properties, making it a promising tool for data unfolding.

title: A Framework for Low-Latency, LLM-driven Multimodal Interaction on the Pepper Robot
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures. To appear in Proceedings of the 21st ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction (HRI '26), Edinburgh, Scotland, March 2026
summary: Despite recent advances in integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) into social robotics, two weaknesses persist. First, existing implementations on platforms like Pepper often rely on cascaded Speech-to-Text (STT)->LLM->Text-to-Speech (TTS) pipelines, resulting in high latency and the loss of paralinguistic information. Second, most implementations fail to fully leverage the LLM's capabilities for multimodal perception and agentic control. We present an open-source Android framework for the Pepper robot that addresses these limitations through two key innovations. First, we integrate end-to-end Speech-to-Speech (S2S) models to achieve low-latency interaction while preserving paralinguistic cues and enabling adaptive intonation. Second, we implement extensive Function Calling capabilities that elevate the LLM to an agentic planner, orchestrating robot actions (navigation, gaze control, tablet interaction) and integrating diverse multimodal feedback (vision, touch, system state). The framework runs on the robot's tablet but can also be built to run on regular Android smartphones or tablets, decoupling development from robot hardware. This work provides the HRI community with a practical, extensible platform for exploring advanced LLM-driven embodied interaction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an Android framework for Pepper robots, incorporating end-to-end Speech-to-Speech models for quicker, more natural interactions, and Function Calling capabilities to enhance planning and multimodal interactivity.
AI.motivation: To address latency problems and the underutilization of LLM capabilities for multimodal perception and agentic control in current robotics systems.
AI.method: Developed an open-source Android framework integrating low-latency Speech-to-Speech models for better paralinguistic communication and Function Calling for comprehensive control and feedback.
AI.result: The framework allows efficient robot communication and advanced LLM-driven capabilities for multimodal interaction, running on Pepper robots' tablets as well as standard Android devices.
AI.conclusion: This framework provides a practical solution for advancing LLM-based social robotics, enabling better interaction quality and development flexibility.

title: Bayesian Learning in Episodic Zero-Sum Games
summary: We study Bayesian learning in episodic, finite-horizon zero-sum Markov games with unknown transition and reward models. We investigate a posterior algorithm in which each player maintains a Bayesian posterior over the game model, independently samples a game model at the beginning of each episode, and computes an equilibrium policy for the sampled model. We analyze two settings: (i) Both players use the posterior sampling algorithm, and (ii) Only one player uses posterior sampling while the opponent follows an arbitrary learning algorithm. In each setting, we provide guarantees on the expected regret of the posterior sampling agent. Our notion of regret compares the expected total reward of the learning agent against the expected total reward under equilibrium policies of the true game. Our main theoretical result is an expected regret bound for the posterior sampling agent of order $O(HS\sqrt{ABHK\log(SABHK)})$ where $K$ is the number of episodes, $H$ is the episode length, $S$ is the number of states, and $A,B$ are the action space sizes of the two players. Experiments in a grid-world predator--prey domain illustrate the sublinear regret scaling and show that posterior sampling competes favorably with a fictitious-play baseline.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes Bayesian learning in finite-horizon zero-sum Markov games and provides regret guarantees for posterior sampling algorithms with both theoretical and experimental validations.
AI.motivation: To understand and provide regret bounds for Bayesian posterior sampling methods in competitive multi-player environments like finite-horizon zero-sum Markov games, where transitions and rewards are unknown.
AI.method: The authors consider a posterior algorithm where one or both players maintain Bayesian posteriors over the game model, sample a model, and compute equilibrium policies. They analyze the resulting regret in two specific settings. Their regret analysis hinges on comparing expected rewards under the learned policy with those under equilibrium policies for the true model.
AI.result: Theoretical results demonstrate an expected regret bound of $O(HS\sqrt{ABHK\log(SABHK)})$, where all relevant game-design variables are encapsulated. Experimental results in a grid-world predator-prey domain corroborate sublinear regret scaling and show competitiveness with a fictitious-play algorithm baseline.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian posterior sampling is a promising approach in learning game dynamics within finite-horizon Markov games, achieving provable regret bounds and competitive experimental performance.

title: RayMap3R: Inference-Time RayMap for Dynamic 3D Reconstruction
summary: Streaming feed-forward 3D reconstruction enables real-time joint estimation of scene geometry and camera poses from RGB images. However, without explicit dynamic reasoning, streaming models can be affected by moving objects, causing artifacts and drift. In this work, we propose RayMap3R, a training-free streaming framework for dynamic scene reconstruction. We observe that RayMap-based predictions exhibit a static-scene bias, providing an internal cue for dynamic identification. Based on this observation, we construct a dual-branch inference scheme that identifies dynamic regions by contrasting RayMap and image predictions, suppressing their interference during memory updates. We further introduce reset metric alignment and state-aware smoothing to preserve metric consistency and stabilize predicted trajectories. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance among streaming approaches on dynamic scene reconstruction across multiple benchmarks.

AI.tldr: RayMap3R is a training-free framework for dynamic 3D scene reconstruction, addressing issues caused by moving objects in real-time geometry and pose estimation.
AI.motivation: Streaming 3D reconstruction struggles with artifacts and drift in dynamic scenes because traditional methods lack explicit dynamic reasoning.
AI.method: The method uses a dual-branch approach to identify dynamic regions by contrasting RayMap predictions with image predictions. It suppresses interference during memory updates and ensures consistency with reset metric alignment and state-aware smoothing.
AI.result: RayMap3R achieves state-of-the-art performance on dynamic scene reconstruction benchmarks, surpassing other streaming approaches.
AI.conclusion: This framework effectively mitigates the challenges in dynamic scene reconstruction, making real-time streaming models more robust to moving objects.

title: From Technical Debt to Cognitive and Intent Debt: Rethinking Software Health in the Age of AI
summary: Over time, the shared understanding that makes a software system safe to change quietly erodes. This gradual loss of understanding across a team increases cognitive debt, while the loss of captured rationale leads to intent debt. These may become more important, than technical debt in AI-assisted software development. This article proposes a triple debt model to reason about software health. It is built around three interacting debt types: technical debt in code, cognitive debt in people, and intent debt in externalized knowledge. Cognitive debt concerns what people understand; intent debt concerns what is explicitly captured for both people and machines to use in the future.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a triple debt model comprising technical, cognitive, and intent debts to evaluate software health.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of maintaining software health by accounting for cognitive and intent debts alongside technical debt, particularly in AI-assisted software development.
AI.method: Introduce a conceptual model that categorizes software debt into three interacting types: technical, cognitive, and intent debts.
AI.result: The triple debt model provides a structured approach to assess and reason about software health.
AI.conclusion: Technical, cognitive, and intent debts collectively impact software health, and addressing all three can improve software maintainability and understanding.

title: HiCI: Hierarchical Construction-Integration for Long-Context Attention
summary: Long-context language modeling is commonly framed as a scalability challenge of token-level attention, yet local-to-global information structuring remains largely implicit in existing approaches. Drawing on cognitive theories of discourse comprehension, we propose HiCI (Hierarchical Construction--Integration), a hierarchical attention module that constructs segment-level representations, integrates them into a shared global context, and broadcasts both to condition segment-level attention. We validate HiCI through parameter-efficient adaptation of LLaMA-2 with only <5.5% additional parameters, extending context from 4K to 100K tokens (7B) and 64K tokens (13B). Across language modeling, retrieval, and instruction-following benchmarks, HiCI yields consistent improvements over strong baselines, including matching proprietary models on topic retrieval and surpassing GPT-3.5-Turbo-16K on code comprehension. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of explicit hierarchical structuring as an inductive bias for long-context modeling.

AI.tldr: HiCI introduces a hierarchical attention mechanism for long-context language modeling, improving performance and extending context size efficiently.
AI.motivation: Current language models face challenges in structuring local-to-global information processing in long-context scenarios.
AI.method: HiCI proposes a hierarchical attention module that builds segment-level representations and uses them to enhance global context understanding.
AI.result: HiCI achieves significant improvements in benchmarks like language modeling and code comprehension compared to strong baselines, while requiring minimal additional parameters.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical structuring with HiCI demonstrates effectiveness as an inductive bias for tackling long-context challenges efficiently.

title: Evaluating Factor-Wise Auxiliary Dynamics Supervision for Latent Structure and Robustness in Simulated Humanoid Locomotion
summary: We evaluate whether factor-wise auxiliary dynamics supervision produces useful latent structure or improved robustness in simulated humanoid locomotion. DynaMITE -- a transformer encoder with a factored 24-d latent trained by per-factor auxiliary losses during proximal policy optimization (PPO) -- is compared against Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), plain Transformer, and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) baselines on a Unitree G1 humanoid across four Isaac Lab tasks. The supervised latent shows no evidence of decodable or functionally separable factor structure: probe R^2 ~ 0 for all five dynamics factors, clamping any subspace changes reward by < 0.05, and standard disentanglement metrics (MIG, DCI, SAP) are near zero. An unsupervised LSTM hidden state achieves higher probe R^2 (up to 0.10). A 2x2 factorial ablation (n = 10 seeds) isolates the contributions of the tanh bottleneck and auxiliary losses: the auxiliary losses show no measurable effect on either in-distribution (ID) reward (+0.03, p = 0.732) or severe out-of-distribution (OOD) reward (+0.03, p = 0.669), while the bottleneck shows a small, consistent advantage in both regimes (ID: +0.16, p = 0.207; OOD: +0.10, p = 0.208). The bottleneck advantage persists under severe combined perturbation but does not amplify, indicating a training-time representation benefit rather than a robustness mechanism. LSTM achieves the best nominal reward on all four tasks (p < 0.03); DynaMITE degrades less under combined-shift stress (2.3% vs. 16.7%), but this difference is attributable to the bottleneck compression, not the auxiliary supervision. For locomotion practitioners: auxiliary dynamics supervision does not produce an interpretable estimator and does not measurably improve reward or robustness beyond what the bottleneck alone provides; recurrent baselines remain the stronger choice for nominal performance.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates DynaMITE, a transformer encoder with auxiliary dynamics supervision, against other baselines for humanoid locomotion tasks, finding no significant benefits from auxiliary dynamics supervision and recommending recurrent baselines for nominal performance.
AI.motivation: To explore whether introducing factor-wise auxiliary dynamics supervision can enhance latent structure learning, interpretability, and robustness in humanoid locomotion tasks.
AI.method: The study compares DynaMITE, featuring a transformer encoder with auxiliary losses, against LSTM, Transformer, and MLP across several tasks on a humanoid platform, using metrics such as probe R^2, disentanglement scores, and rewards under different perturbation conditions.
AI.result: Auxiliary dynamics supervision did not yield measurable improvements in interpretability, ID/OOD rewards, or robustness. The tanh bottleneck provided some training-time benefit, and LSTM consistently achieved better nominal performance. DynaMITE showed less degradation under stress due to the bottleneck, not auxiliary supervision.
AI.conclusion: Auxiliary dynamics supervision for latent structure does not improve performance or robustness in humanoid locomotion tasks. LSTMs are recommended for practitioners due to their superior baseline performance.

title: Sparse Weak-Form Discovery of Stochastic Generators
summary: We introduce a framework for the data-driven discovery of stochastic differential equations (SDEs) that unifies, for the first time, the weak-form integration-by-parts approach of Weak SINDy with the stochastic system identification goal of stochastic SINDy. The central novelty is the adoption of spatial Gaussian test functions $K_j(x)=\exp(-|x-x_j|^2/2h^2)$ in place of temporal test functions. Because the kernel weight $K_j(X_{t_n})$ is $\mathcal{F}_{t_n}$-measurable and the Brownian innovation $ξ_n$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_{t_n}$, every noise term in the projected response has zero conditional mean given the current state -- a property that guarantees unbiasedness in expectation and prevents the structural regression bias that afflicts temporal test functions in the stochastic setting. This design choice converts the SDE identification problem into two sparse linear systems -- one for the drift $b(x)$ and one for the diffusion tensor $a(x)$ -- that share a single design matrix and are solved jointly via $\ell_1$-regularised regression with grouped cross-validation. A two-step bias-correction procedure handles state-dependent diffusion. Validated on the Ornstein--Uhlenbeck process, the double-well Langevin system, and a multiplicative diffusion process, the method recovers all active polynomial generators with coefficient errors below 4\%, stationary-density total-variation distances below 0.01, and autocorrelation functions that faithfully reproduce true relaxation timescales across all three benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel framework combining the advantages of Weak SINDy and stochastic SINDy to identify stochastic differential equations (SDEs) using spatial Gaussian test functions.
AI.motivation: To address structural regression bias in identifying SDEs by adopting spatial Gaussian test functions, ensuring unbiasedness and better accuracy in stochastic system identification.
AI.method: The method converts the identification of SDEs into two sparse linear systems for drift and diffusion components, solved using $ell_1$-regularised regression and a two-step bias-correction approach.
AI.result: Validated on three benchmark systems, the framework achieved coefficient errors below 4%, stationary-density total-variation distances below 0.01, and accurate autocorrelation functions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively and accurately identifies SDEs by mitigating regression bias, yielding reliable results across diverse benchmark systems.

title: Knowledge Boundary Discovery for Large Language Models
summary: We propose Knowledge Boundary Discovery (KBD), a reinforcement learning based framework to explore the knowledge boundaries of the Large Language Models (LLMs). We define the knowledge boundary by automatically generating two types of questions: (i) those the LLM can confidently answer (within-knowledge boundary) and (ii) those it cannot (beyond-knowledge boundary). Iteratively exploring and exploiting the LLM's responses to find its knowledge boundaries is challenging because of the hallucination phenomenon. To find the knowledge boundaries of an LLM, the agent interacts with the LLM under the modeling of exploring a partially observable environment. The agent generates a progressive question as the action, adopts an entropy reduction as the reward, receives the LLM's response as the observation and updates its belief states. We demonstrate that the KBD detects knowledge boundaries of LLMs by automatically finding a set of non-trivial answerable and unanswerable questions. We validate the KBD by comparing its generated knowledge boundaries with manually crafted LLM benchmark datasets. Experiments show that our KBD-generated question set is comparable to the human-generated datasets. Our approach paves a new way to evaluate LLMs.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework, Knowledge Boundary Discovery (KBD), to find the limits of Large Language Models' (LLMs) knowledge by generating questions the model can and cannot confidently answer.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of identifying the boundaries of LLMs' knowledge in light of their hallucination phenomenon and to create an automated evaluation method for LLMs.
AI.method: The method involves using reinforcement learning where an agent progressively generates questions, evaluates LLM responses as observations, and iteratively updates its belief states to identify knowledge boundaries in a partially observable environment.
AI.result: The KBD framework produces sets of answerable and unanswerable questions that are comparable to human-crafted benchmark datasets, effectively identifying LLMs' knowledge boundaries.
AI.conclusion: KBD is a novel and effective method to automatically evaluate LLM capabilities, providing an alternative to manually-created benchmarks and enabling better understanding of LLMs.

title: Beyond Token Eviction: Mixed-Dimension Budget Allocation for Efficient KV Cache Compression
summary: Key-value (KV) caching is widely used to accelerate transformer inference, but its memory cost grows linearly with input length, limiting long-context deployment. Existing token eviction methods reduce memory by discarding less important tokens, which can be viewed as a coarse form of dimensionality reduction that assigns each token either zero or full dimension. We propose MixedDimKV, a mixed-dimension KV cache compression method that allocates dimensions to tokens at a more granular level, and MixedDimKV-H, which further integrates head-level importance information. Experiments on long-context benchmarks show that MixedDimKV outperforms prior KV cache compression methods that do not rely on head-level importance profiling. When equipped with the same head-level importance information, MixedDimKV-H consistently outperforms HeadKV. Notably, our approach achieves comparable performance to full attention on LongBench with only 6.25% of the KV cache. Furthermore, in the Needle-in-a-Haystack test, our solution maintains 100% accuracy at a 50K context length while using as little as 0.26% of the cache.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MixedDimKV and MixedDimKV-H, advanced methods for compressing KV cache in transformer inference, achieving significant memory savings while maintaining high accuracy.
AI.motivation: The increasing memory cost associated with KV caching in long-context transformer inference limits its scalability and practical application.
AI.method: The authors propose MixedDimKV and MixedDimKV-H, which use granular token importance profiling and head-level importance integration to compress KV cache, as opposed to prior coarse token eviction methods.
AI.result: MixedDimKV outperforms prior KV cache compression methods. MixedDimKV-H improves further by leveraging head-level importance, achieving comparable performance to full attention benchmarks with drastically reduced memory usage.
AI.conclusion: MixedDimKV and MixedDimKV-H significantly enhance transformer inference efficiency, enabling long-context applications with minimal KV cache memory requirements while maintaining high performance.

title: GaussianPile: A Unified Sparse Gaussian Splatting Framework for Slice-based Volumetric Reconstruction
comment: Accepted by IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026 (CVPR 2026)
summary: Slice-based volumetric imaging is widely applied and it demands representations that compress aggressively while preserving internal structure for analysis. We introduce GaussianPile, unifying 3D Gaussian splatting with an imaging system-aware focus model to address this challenge. Our proposed method introduces three key innovations: (i) a slice-aware piling strategy that positions anisotropic 3D Gaussians to model through-slice contributions, (ii) a differentiable projection operator that encodes the finite-thickness point spread function of the imaging acquisition system, and (iii) a compact encoding and joint optimization pipeline that simultaneously reconstructs and compresses the Gaussian sets. Our CUDA-based design retains the compression and real-time rendering efficiency of Gaussian primitives while preserving high-frequency internal volumetric detail. Experiments on microscopy and ultrasound datasets demonstrate that our method reduces storage and reconstruction cost, sustains diagnostic fidelity, and enables fast 2D visualization, along with 3D voxelization. In practice, it delivers high-quality results in as few as 3 minutes, up to 11x faster than NeRF-based approaches, and achieves consistent 16x compression over voxel grids, offering a practical path to deployable compression and exploration of slice-based volumetric datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GaussianPile, a system for compressing and visualizing slice-based volumetric imaging efficiently while preserving detail and diagnostic fidelity.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in compressing and rendering slice-based volumetric imaging data while retaining high internal structure fidelity for diagnostics and analysis.
AI.method: The approach involves (i) a slice-aware piling strategy for anisotropic 3D Gaussians, (ii) a differentiable projection operator encoding the imaging system's finite-thickness point spread function, and (iii) a compact encoding and joint optimization framework to reconstruct and compress Gaussian sets efficiently.
AI.result: GaussianPile achieves significant compression (16x over voxel grids), high-quality reconstruction, and real-time rendering at faster speeds (up to 11x faster than NeRF-based alternatives), validated on microscopy and ultrasound datasets.
AI.conclusion: The method successfully balances compression and rendering speed with diagnostic fidelity, providing a deployable and practical solution for visualizing and exploring slice-based volumetric datasets.

title: Lemma Discovery in Agentic Program Verification
summary:
Deductive verification provides strong correctness guarantees for code by extracting verification conditions (VCs) and writing formal proofs for them. The expertise-intensive task of VC proving is the main bottleneck in this process, and has been partly automated owing to recent advances in Large Language Model (LLM) agents. However, existing proof agents are not able to discover helper lemmas - auxiliary lemmas that aid in proving - and thus fall short as programs grow in size and complexity.
  In this paper, we argue that VC proving for program verification is more than a purely mathematical task, and benefits considerably from program comprehension. Our key insight is that human-proof engineers often discover and apply helper lemmas based on their understanding of the program semantics, which are not directly reflected in the VCs produced by VC generators. Inspired by this insight, we propose an LLM agent, LemmaNet, that discovers helper lemmas in two ways. Specifically, the agent first synthesizes lemmas offline by directly analyzing the source code and specifications, and then relating this semantic understanding to the mechanical, verbose encoding produced by VC generators. As the proof unfolds, LemmaNet then adapts existing helper lemmas online to accommodate evolving proof states, enabling the agent to effectively discharge complex VCs on-the-fly.
  We evaluate LemmaNet on SV-COMP and established real-world subjects, including modules of the Linux kernel, Contiki OS, standard C++ library, and X.509 parser. Our experimental results demonstrate that LemmaNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, highlighting the importance of program comprehension-aided lemma discovery in agentic program verification.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LemmaNet, an LLM-based agent designed to improve deductive program verification by discovering helper lemmas through program comprehension. Evaluation shows superior performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: The key challenge in deductive verification lies in automating the task of proving verification conditions, which traditionally requires human-provided helper lemmas and expertise. Current methods fall short as programs increase in complexity.
AI.method: The proposed LemmaNet agent synthesizes helper lemmas by analyzing the source code offline with its semantic understanding, and adapts them online during unfolding proof states to discharge complex verification conditions dynamically.
AI.result: LemmaNet was tested on SV-COMP and real-world codebases, including Linux kernel modules and C++ libraries. It consistently outperformed state-of-the-art approaches in VC proving tasks.
AI.conclusion: Program comprehension plays a vital role in enhancing automated program verification. LemmaNet successfully integrates helper lemma discovery and proves to be effective in complex program scenarios.

title: Can ChatGPT Really Understand Modern Chinese Poetry?
summary: ChatGPT has demonstrated remarkable capabilities on both poetry generation and translation, yet its ability to truly understand poetry remains unexplored. Previous poetry-related work merely analyzed experimental outcomes without addressing fundamental issues of comprehension. This paper introduces a comprehensive framework for evaluating ChatGPT's understanding of modern poetry. We collaborated with professional poets to evaluate ChatGPT's interpretation of modern Chinese poems by different poets along multiple dimensions. Evaluation results show that ChatGPT's interpretations align with the original poets' intents in over 73% of the cases. However, its understanding in certain dimensions, particularly in capturing poeticity, proved to be less satisfactory. These findings highlight the effectiveness and necessity of our proposed framework. This study not only evaluates ChatGPT's ability to understand modern poetry but also establishes a solid foundation for future research on LLMs and their application to poetry-related tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper assesses ChatGPT's ability to comprehend modern Chinese poetry, using evaluations by professional poets, and proposes a framework for such analysis.
AI.motivation: To explore and evaluate ChatGPT's capacity for interpreting modern poetry, an ability that has remained largely unexplored despite its competence in poetry generation and translation.
AI.method: Developed a comprehensive evaluation framework and engaged professional poets to assess ChatGPT’s interpretations across various dimensions of modern Chinese poetry.
AI.result: ChatGPT's interpretations aligned with original poets’ intentions in over 73% of cases, but performed poorly in capturing poeticity.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed framework and emphasizes the need for future research on LLMs’ nuanced understanding of poetry.

title: DyGeoVLN: Infusing Dynamic Geometry Foundation Model into Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to understand visual observations and language instructions to navigate in unseen environments. Most existing approaches rely on static scene assumptions and struggle to generalize in dynamic, real-world scenarios. To address this challenge, we propose DyGeoVLN, a dynamic geometry-aware VLN framework. Our method infuses a dynamic geometry foundation model into the VLN framework through cross-branch feature fusion to enable explicit 3D spatial representation and visual-semantic reasoning. To efficiently compress historical token information in long-horizon, dynamic navigation, we further introduce a novel pose-free and adaptive-resolution token-pruning strategy. This strategy can remove spatio-temporal redundant tokens to reduce inference cost. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks and exhibits strong robustness in real-world environments.

AI.tldr: DyGeoVLN is a framework for Vision-language Navigation (VLN) that introduces dynamic geometry-awareness and a token-pruning strategy for improved performance in dynamic and real-world environments.
AI.motivation: Current VLN methods lack generalizability in dynamic or real-world scenarios due to their reliance on static scene assumptions.
AI.method: The paper integrates a dynamic geometry foundation model into VLN through cross-branch feature fusion and introduces a pose-free, adaptive-resolution token-pruning strategy to compress historical token information efficiently.
AI.result: Experiments show that DyGeoVLN achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks and demonstrates high robustness in dynamic, real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses the challenges of VLN in dynamic environments, enabling better 3D spatial awareness and reducing inference costs while maintaining high performance.

title: Bayesian Scattering: A Principled Baseline for Uncertainty on Image Data
summary: Uncertainty quantification for image data is dominated by complex deep learning methods, yet the field lacks an interpretable, mathematically grounded baseline. We propose Bayesian scattering to fill this gap, serving as a first-step baseline akin to the role of Bayesian linear regression for tabular data. Our method couples the wavelet scattering transform-a deep, non-learned feature extractor-with a simple probabilistic head. Because scattering features are derived from geometric principles rather than learned, they avoid overfitting the training distribution. This helps provide sensible uncertainty estimates even under significant distribution shifts. We validate this on diverse tasks, including medical imaging under institution shift, wealth mapping under country-to-country shift, and Bayesian optimization of molecular properties. Our results suggest that Bayesian scattering is a solid baseline for complex uncertainty quantification methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Bayesian scattering as a baseline for uncertainty quantification, combining wavelet scattering transform with probabilistic modeling to yield interpretable uncertainty estimations, especially under distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: To provide an interpretable and mathematically grounded baseline for uncertainty quantification in image data, which is currently dominated by complex deep learning methods.
AI.method: The method combines wavelet scattering transform (a non-learned, geometric-based feature extractor) with a probabilistic head for deriving uncertainty estimates.
AI.result: Bayesian scattering was validated across tasks like medical imaging (institution shift), wealth mapping (country-to-country shift), and molecular property optimization, showing effective uncertainty quantification even in distribution-shift scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian scattering is a versatile and interpretable baseline, offering mathematically grounded and reliable uncertainty quantification, suitable for a variety of complex tasks.

title: KLDrive: Fine-Grained 3D Scene Reasoning for Autonomous Driving based on Knowledge Graph
summary: Autonomous driving requires reliable reasoning over fine-grained 3D scene facts. Fine-grained question answering over multi-modal driving observations provides a natural way to evaluate this capability, yet existing perception pipelines and driving-oriented large language model (LLM) methods still suffer from unreliable scene facts, hallucinations, opaque reasoning, and heavy reliance on task-specific training. We present KLDrive, the first knowledge-graph-augmented LLM reasoning framework for fine-grained question answering in autonomous driving. KLDrive addresses this problem through designing two tightly coupled components: an energy-based scene fact construction module that consolidates multi-source evidence into a reliable scene knowledge graph, and an LLM agent that performs fact-grounded reasoning over a constrained action space under explicit structural constraints. By combining structured prompting with few-shot in-context exemplars, the framework adapts to diverse reasoning tasks without heavy task-specific fine-tuning. Experiments on two large-scale autonomous-driving QA benchmarks show that KLDrive outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods, achieving the best overall accuracy of 65.04% on NuScenes-QA and the best SPICE score of 42.45 on GVQA. On counting, the most challenging factual reasoning task, it improves over the strongest baseline by 46.01 percentage points, demonstrating substantially reduced hallucinations and the benefit of coupling reliable scene fact construction with explicit reasoning.

AI.tldr: KLDrive is a new LLM framework augmented with a knowledge graph for fine-grained question answering in autonomous driving, addressing issues like hallucinations and unreliable scene facts while achieving state-of-the-art results on QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving demands precise reasoning over detailed 3D scene data, but current methods struggle with inaccuracies, hallucinations, and reliance on domain-specific training during question answering over multi-modal driving observations.
AI.method: KLDrive utilizes an energy-based module to consolidate scene facts into a knowledge graph and an LLM agent for structured, fact-grounded reasoning, with minimal task-specific fine-tuning, leveraging few-shot prompting.
AI.result: KLDrive achieved state-of-the-art performance with a 65.04% accuracy on NuScenes-QA, a 42.45 SPICE score on GVQA benchmarks, and a 46.01 percentage point improvement in counting accuracy over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: KLDrive significantly enhances reasoning in autonomous driving by effectively combining reliable scene fact construction with explicit reasoning, reducing hallucination issues and achieving superior QA benchmark results.

title: Optimal low-rank stochastic gradient estimation for LLM training
summary: Large language model (LLM) training is often bottlenecked by memory constraints and stochastic gradient noise in extremely high-dimensional parameter spaces. Motivated by empirical evidence that many LLM gradient matrices are effectively low-rank during training, we present an unbiased, memory-efficient, low-rank matrix estimator with the lowest variance that is applicable across common stochastic gradient estimation paradigms. The core idea is to project a high-dimensional stochastic gradient estimator onto a random low-dimensional subspace and lift it back, reducing memory while keeping the estimator unbiased and controlling mean-squared error via an optimally designed projection distribution, including Haar--Stiefel projections. The projection distribution is derived by solving a constrained functional optimization problem, yielding an optimal random projector that guides algorithm design. Empirically, the resulting low-rank gradient estimators deliver both practical memory savings and improved training behavior. In RoBERTa-large fine-tuning, our method attains the lowest peak GPU memory among compared methods (e.g., 3.83GB versus 16.7GB for full BP) while remaining competitive in accuracy; in autoregressive LLM pretraining (LLaMA-20M/60M/100M), our method outperforms the traditional methods, supporting the benefit of the proposed optimal projection strategy.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a low-rank matrix estimator to reduce memory usage and noise in training large language models, achieving significant memory savings and improved training efficiency.
AI.motivation: Training large language models is constrained by high memory demands and stochastic gradient noise in large parameter spaces.
AI.method: A low-rank matrix estimator is proposed, involving random projections to reduce dimensionality while maintaining unbiasedness and minimizing error, optimized using constrained functional optimization.
AI.result: The method reduces peak GPU memory usage (e.g., 3.83GB vs. 16.7GB in RoBERTa-large fine-tuning) and improves training accuracy, outperforming traditional methods in autoregressive LLM pretraining.
AI.conclusion: The proposed projection strategy offers practical memory savings and enhances training outcomes, demonstrating its utility in fine-tuning and pretraining large language models.

title: ScaleEdit-12M: Scaling Open-Source Image Editing Data Generation via Multi-Agent Framework
summary: Instruction-based image editing has emerged as a key capability for unified multimodal models (UMMs), yet constructing large-scale, diverse, and high-quality editing datasets without costly proprietary APIs remains challenging. Previous image editing datasets either rely on closed-source models for annotation, which prevents cost-effective scaling, or employ fixed synthetic editing pipelines, which suffer from limited quality and generalizability. To address these challenges, we propose ScaleEditor, a fully open-source hierarchical multi-agent framework for end-to-end construction of large-scale, high-quality image editing datasets. Our pipeline consists of three key components: source image expansion with world-knowledge infusion, adaptive multi-agent editing instruction-image synthesis, and a task-aware data quality verification mechanism. Using ScaleEditor, we curate ScaleEdit-12M, the largest open-source image editing dataset to date, spanning 23 task families across diverse real and synthetic domains. Fine-tuning UniWorld-V1 and Bagel on ScaleEdit yields consistent gains, improving performance by up to 10.4% on ImgEdit and 35.1% on GEdit for general editing benchmarks and by up to 150.0% on RISE and 26.5% on KRIS-Bench for knowledge-infused benchmarks. These results demonstrate that open-source, agentic pipelines can approach commercial-grade data quality while retaining cost-effectiveness and scalability. Both the framework and dataset will be open-sourced.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ScaleEditor, an open-source framework for constructing large-scale, high-quality image editing datasets without costly proprietary APIs. Using this method, they create ScaleEdit-12M, the largest open-source dataset, and achieve significant performance improvements in various benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to tackle the challenges of constructing expansive and high-quality image editing datasets without relying on expensive proprietary APIs or limited synthetic pipelines.
AI.method: The authors develop ScaleEditor, a hierarchical multi-agent framework with three key stages: 1) source image expansion with world-knowledge integration, 2) adaptive multi-agent editing instruction and image synthesis, and 3) a task-aware data quality verification mechanism.
AI.result: The use of ScaleEditor produces ScaleEdit-12M, a 12-million-instance dataset covering 23 task families. Fine-tuning models with this dataset yields performance improvements up to 150% on knowledge-infused benchmarks and up to 35.1% on general editing tasks.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that open-source, agentic pipelines like ScaleEditor can achieve near-commercial-grade data quality while being scalable and cost-effective. Additionally, the framework and dataset will be open-sourced to benefit the community.

title: Byte-level Object Bounds Protection
summary:
Low-level C programs remain highly vulnerable to out-of-bounds memory corruption. State-of-the-art precise defenses either introduce severe runtime overhead due to metadata memory lookups, or break standard C semantics by disallowing partial structs or the creation of an object's end address (EA), a legal operation ubiquitous in real-world C code. Conversely, practical alignment-based solutions achieve efficiency only by relaxing protected bounds.
  We present PRISM, a precise, zero-lookup object-bounds scheme that eliminates these restrictions. PRISM compresses a 47-bit EA into the 17-bit unused tag area of a 64-bit pointer. By enforcing the invariant that a statically known starting address (KSA) cannot exceed the EA, PRISM completely eliminates the need for costly metadata memory fetches in nearly all bounds checks, while strictly retaining precise object bounds. Our invariant also simplifies the lower-bound checks in existing alignment-based solutions, thus improving their performance.
  To achieve high throughput, PRISM introduces q-padding, an optimization that safely removes bounds checks for constant-offset accesses (such as struct fields) while maintaining precise, byte-level protection for the variable-indexed accesses primarily exploited by attackers.
  Evaluated on SPEC 2017, PRISM achieves an arithmetic mean CPU overhead of 46.1\% with a 32-byte q-padding (dropping to 31.3\% in a 32-bit address space). On highly concurrent, real-world workloads, PRISM secures a fully saturated Apache web server with only an 11.1\% throughput reduction, demonstrating its readiness for production deployment. Furthermore, PRISM successfully detected an out-of-bounds violation in \texttt{gcc} that prior tools missed due to their lack of support for partial structs.

AI.tldr: PRISM is a new approach for mitigating out-of-bounds memory corruption in C programs, offering precise protection without incurring severe overhead or breaking C semantics.
AI.motivation: Current defenses against out-of-bounds memory corruption either introduce high runtime cost, compromise C language semantics, or reduce protection precision, necessitating a practical and efficient solution.
AI.method: PRISM compresses metadata into unused pointer tags and introduces "q-padding" optimization to reduce runtime overhead while preserving robust bounds checks. It achieves efficient memory safety without expensive metadata lookups.
AI.result: The PRISM system demonstrated robust, byte-level protection with notably reduced runtime overhead (e.g., 46.1% on SPEC 2017 benchmarks). Real-world tests (e.g., Apache server) showed a minimal 11.1% performance impact and detected errors missed by existing tools.
AI.conclusion: PRISM offers precise and efficient object-bounds enforcement that is ready for deployment, addressing both performance and semantic compatibility concerns in real-world scenarios.

title: SozKZ: Training Efficient Small Language Models for Kazakh from Scratch
summary: Kazakh, a Turkic language spoken by over 22 million people, remains underserved by existing multilingual language models, which allocate minimal capacity to low-resource languages and employ tokenizers ill-suited to agglutinative morphology. We present SozKZ, a family of Llama-architecture language models (50M-600M parameters) trained entirely from scratch on 9 billion tokens of Kazakh text with a dedicated 50K BPE tokenizer. We evaluate all models on three Kazakh benchmarks -- multiple-choice cultural QA, reading comprehension (Belebele), and topic classification (SIB-200) -- alongside five multilingual baselines ranging from 500M to 3B parameters. Our 600M model achieves 30.3% accuracy on Kazakh cultural QA, approaching the 32.0% of Llama-3.2-1B (2x larger), and 25.5% on SIB-200 topic classification, surpassing all evaluated multilingual models up to 2B parameters. We observe consistent scaling from 50M to 600M, with MC QA accuracy rising from 22.8% to 30.3%, suggesting that further scaling remains beneficial. These results demonstrate that small, dedicated models trained from scratch with a language-appropriate tokenizer offer a viable path for low-resource language technology, achieving competitive performance at a fraction of the computational cost. All models and the tokenizer are released under open licenses.

AI.tldr: SozKZ introduces Kazakh-focused language models based on the Llama architecture, with parameter sizes of 50M-600M. These models are trained from scratch using a Kazakh-specific tokenizer on 9 billion tokens, achieving competitive results on several benchmarks compared to larger multilingual models.
AI.motivation: Kazakh, as a low-resource Turkic language, lacks effective representation in existing multilingual language models. This gap motivates the development of dedicated models tailored to Kazakh's agglutinative morphology and linguistic structure.
AI.method: The authors train a family of Llama-like models (50M-600M parameters) from scratch using a 50K BPE tokenizer specific to Kazakh on a dataset of 9 billion tokens. Their performance is evaluated on three Kazakh language benchmarks and compared to larger multilingual models.
AI.result: The 600M-parameter model achieves notable performance, scoring 30.3% on Kazakh cultural QA (close to larger models' 32.0%) and 25.5% on SIB-200 topic classification, surpassing multilingual models up to 2B parameters. Consistent scaling benefits are observed across smaller model versions.
AI.conclusion: Small and specialized models tailored to low-resource languages like Kazakh, trained from scratch with appropriate tokenizers, can achieve competitive performance while being computationally efficient. The output is openly available for further use and research.

title: Bayesian Active Object Recognition and 6D Pose Estimation from Multimodal Contact Sensing
summary: We present an active tactile exploration framework for joint object recognition and 6D pose estimation. The proposed method integrates wrist force/torque sensing, GelSight tactile sensing, and free-space constraints within a Bayesian inference framework that maintains a belief over object class and pose during active tactile exploration. By combining contact and non-contact evidence, the framework reduces ambiguity and improves robustness in the joint class-pose estimation problem. To enable efficient inference in the large hypothesis space, we employ a customized particle filter that progressively samples particles based on new observations. The inferred belief is further used to guide active exploration by selecting informative next touches under reachability constraints.
