For effective data collection, a motion planning and control framework is developed to plan and execute feasible paths for tactile exploration, handle unexpected contacts and GelSight-surface alignment with tactile servoing. We evaluate the framework in simulation and on a Franka Panda robot using 11 YCB objects. Results show that incorporating tactile and free-space information substantially improves recognition and pose estimation accuracy and stability, while reducing the number of action cycles compared with force/torque-only baselines. Code, dataset, and supplementary material will be made available online.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework that uses tactile and force/torque sensing combined with Bayesian inference for object recognition and 6D pose estimation, showing improved accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: The study aims to tackle the challenge of combining tactile and contact-less sensing to improve object recognition and 6D pose estimation, reducing ambiguities and increasing robustness.
AI.method: The authors utilize a Bayesian inference framework with a customized particle filter to integrate tactile sensing, wrist force/torque data, and free-space constraints. Active exploration is guided by informative touch selection under reachability constraints.
AI.result: Experiments with simulation and a robot (Franka Panda) demonstrate enhanced accuracy, pose estimation stability, and reduced action cycles compared with sensor-only baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively combines tactile and free-space sensing for efficient object recognition and pose estimation, offering advancements in robotic exploration capabilities.

title: Two Approaches to Direct Estimation of Riesz Representers
comment: A short technical and historical note
summary: The Riesz representer is a central object in semiparametric statistics and debiased/doubly-robust estimation. Two literatures in econometrics have highlighted the role for directly estimating Riesz representers: the automatic debiased machine learning literature (as in Chernozhukov et al., 2022b), and an independent literature on sieve methods for conditional moment models (as in Chen et al., 2014). These two literatures solve distinct optimization problems that in the population both have the Riesz representer as their solution. We show that with unregularized or ridge-regularized linear, sieve, or RKHS models, the two resulting estimators are numerically equivalent. However, for other regularization schemes such as the Lasso, or more general machine learning function classes including neural networks, the estimators are not necessarily equivalent. In the latter case, the Chen et al. (2014) formulation yields a novel constrained optimization problem for directly estimating Riesz representers with machine learning. Drawing on results from Birrell et al. (2022), we conjecture that this approach may offer statistical advantages at the cost of greater computational complexity.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes two econometric approaches to estimate Riesz representers, emphasizing their equivalence under certain regularizations and proposing a novel optimization method for machine learning models.
AI.motivation: To explore how different estimation approaches for the Riesz representer are connected and to propose a novel optimization method when using advanced machine learning models.
AI.method: Comparison of methods under linear, sieve, RKHS models, and advanced machine learning function classes.
AI.result: Equivalence of estimators under some regularizations; a new constrained optimization problem is identified for advanced ML models, potentially offering advantages.
AI.conclusion: The proposed optimization approach may improve estimation with trade-offs in computational costs when using nonstandard ML models.

title: LongCat-Flash-Prover: Advancing Native Formal Reasoning via Agentic Tool-Integrated Reinforcement Learning
summary: We introduce LongCat-Flash-Prover, a flagship 560-billion-parameter open-source Mixture-of- Experts (MoE) model that advances Native Formal Reasoning in Lean4 through agentic tool-integrated reasoning (TIR). We decompose the native formal reasoning task into three independent formal capabilities, i.e., auto-formalization, sketching, and proving. To facilitate these capabilities, we propose a Hybrid-Experts Iteration Framework to expand high-quality task trajectories, including generating a formal statement based on a given informal problem, producing a whole-proof directly from the statement, or a lemma-style sketch. During agentic RL, we present a Hierarchical Importance Sampling Policy Optimization (HisPO) algorithm, which aims to stabilize the MoE model training on such long-horizon tasks. It employs a gradient masking strategy that accounts for the policy staleness and the inherent train-inference engine discrepancies at both sequence and token levels. Additionally, we also incorporate theorem consistency and legality detection mechanisms to eliminate reward hacking issues. Extensive evaluations show that our LongCat-Flash-Prover sets a new state-of-the-art for open-weights models in both auto-formalization and theorem proving. Demonstrating remarkable sample efficiency, it achieves a 97.1% pass rate on MiniF2F-Test using only 72 inference budget per problem. On more challenging benchmarks, it solves 70.8% of ProverBench and 41.5% of PutnamBench with no more than 220 attempts per problem, significantly outperforming existing open-weights baselines.

AI.tldr: Introduction of LongCat-Flash-Prover, a 560B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model for formal reasoning in Lean4, with state-of-the-art performance on theorem proving and auto-formalization benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To advance Native Formal Reasoning in Lean4 by addressing the challenges of auto-formalization, sketching, and proving with an agentic tool-integrated reasoning framework.
AI.method: A Hybrid-Experts Iteration Framework for task trajectory expansion, Hierarchical Importance Sampling Policy Optimization (HisPO) to stabilize training, and theorem consistency and legality detection mechanisms to ensure model reliability.
AI.result: LongCat-Flash-Prover achieves 97.1% pass rate on MiniF2F-Test and excels on ProverBench and PutnamBench with significant performance improvements over open-weight baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods set a new state-of-the-art in formal reasoning tasks and demonstrate the potential for highly efficient theorem proving and auto-formalization using open-source models.

title: CFNN: Continued Fraction Neural Network
summary: Accurately characterizing non-linear functional manifolds with singularities is a fundamental challenge in scientific computing. While Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) dominate, their spectral bias hinders resolving high-curvature features without excessive parameters. We introduce Continued Fraction Neural Networks (CFNNs), integrating continued fractions with gradient-based optimization to provide a ``rational inductive bias.'' This enables capturing complex asymptotics and discontinuities with extreme parameter frugality. We provide formal approximation bounds demonstrating exponential convergence and stability guarantees. To address recursive instability, we develop three implementations: CFNN-Boost, CFNN-MoE, and CFNN-Hybrid. Benchmarks show CFNNs consistently outperform MLPs in precision with one to two orders of magnitude fewer parameters, exhibiting up to a 47-fold improvement in noise robustness and physical consistency. By bridging black-box flexibility and white-box transparency, CFNNs establish a reliable ``grey-box'' paradigm for AI-driven scientific research.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Continued Fraction Neural Networks (CFNNs) to address limitations of Multi-Layer Perceptrons in capturing complex features, offering greater precision, robustness, and efficiency.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome MLPs' spectral bias, which inhibits capturing high-curvature features without excessive parameters.
AI.method: The paper proposes CFNNs, incorporating continued fractions with gradient-based optimization to capture complex asymptotics and discontinuities with fewer parameters.
AI.result: CFNNs outperform MLPs in precision, use fewer parameters, exhibit improved noise robustness, and maintain physical consistency.
AI.conclusion: CFNNs merge flexibility and transparency, offering a "grey-box" paradigm for scientific computing with exponential convergence and stability.

title: A Multihead Continual Learning Framework for Fine-Grained Fashion Image Retrieval with Contrastive Learning and Exponential Moving Average Distillation
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Multimedia (TMM), to appear. Preprint version
summary: Most fine-grained fashion image retrieval (FIR) methods assume a static setting, requiring full retraining when new attributes appear, which is costly and impractical for dynamic scenarios. Although pretrained models support zero-shot inference, their accuracy drops without supervision, and no prior work explores class-incremental learning (CIL) for fine-grained FIR. We propose a multihead continual learning framework for fine-grained fashion image retrieval with contrastive learning and exponential moving average (EMA) distillation (MCL-FIR). MCL-FIR adopts a multi-head design to accommodate evolving classes across increments, reformulates triplet inputs into doublets with InfoNCE for simpler and more effective training, and employs EMA distillation for efficient knowledge transfer. Experiments across four datasets demonstrate that, beyond its scalability, MCL-FIR achieves a strong balance between efficiency and accuracy. It significantly outperforms CIL baselines under similar training cost, and compared with static methods, it delivers comparable performance while using only about 30% of the training cost. The source code is publicly available in https://github.com/Dr-LingXiao/MCL-FIR.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MCL-FIR, a class-incremental learning framework for fine-grained fashion image retrieval, addressing scalability issues in dynamic scenarios.
AI.motivation: Static fine-grained fashion image retrieval methods are costly and impractical for dynamic scenarios, while zero-shot methods lack accuracy, creating the need for an efficient class-incremental approach.
AI.method: The authors propose MCL-FIR, which uses a multi-head continual learning framework with contrastive learning, InfoNCE reformulation of inputs, and EMA distillation for scalable and efficient training.
AI.result: Experiments on four datasets show that MCL-FIR significantly outperforms class-incremental learning baselines with similar costs and achieves comparable accuracy to static methods at 30% of their training cost.
AI.conclusion: MCL-FIR provides a scalable, efficient, and accurate solution for fine-grained fashion image retrieval in evolving, dynamic scenarios.

title: SkillProbe: Security Auditing for Emerging Agent Skill Marketplaces via Multi-Agent Collaboration
summary: With the rapid evolution of Large Language Model (LLM) agent ecosystems, centralized skill marketplaces have emerged as pivotal infrastructure for augmenting agent capabilities. However, these marketplaces face unprecedented security challenges, primarily stemming from semantic-behavioral inconsistency and inter-skill combinatorial risks, where individually benign skills induce malicious behaviors during collaborative invocation. To address these vulnerabilities, we propose SkillProbe, a multi-stage security auditing framework driven by multi-agent collaboration. SkillProbe introduces a "Skills-for-Skills" design paradigm, encapsulating auditing processes into standardized skill modules to drive specialized agents through a rigorous pipeline, including admission filtering, semantic-behavioral alignment detection, and combinatorial risk simulation. We conducted a large-scale evaluation using 8 mainstream LLM series across 2,500 real-world skills from ClawHub. Our results reveal a striking popularity-security paradox, where download volume is not a reliable proxy for security quality, as over 90% of high-popularity skills failed to pass rigorous auditing. Crucially, we discovered that high-risk skills form a single giant connected component within the risk-link dimension, demonstrating that cascaded risks are systemic rather than isolated occurrences. We hope that SkillProbe will inspire researchers to provide a scalable governance infrastructure for constructing a trustworthy Agentic Web. SkillProbe is accessible for public experience at skillhub.holosai.io.

AI.tldr: The study introduces SkillProbe, a framework to address security risks in LLM skill marketplaces via multi-agent collaboration for auditing and testing skill behaviors.
AI.motivation: The rapid growth of LLM agent ecosystems has brought security concerns to skill marketplaces, particularly issues like semantic-behavioral inconsistencies and risks from combining benign skills. A more robust security framework is needed.
AI.method: The researchers developed SkillProbe, a multi-stage auditing system featuring standardized skill modules that test skills for admission filtering, semantic-behavioral alignment, and combinatorial risk simulation. The process is powered by multi-agent collaboration.
AI.result: The framework was tested on 8 LLM series and evaluated 2,500 real-world skills. Results highlighted systemic risks, including that over 90% of popular skills failed security audits and high-risk skills were interconnected within a risk-link network.
AI.conclusion: SkillProbe can serve as an essential step towards scalable governance infrastructure for ensuring security and trustworthiness in LLM skill marketplaces, encouraging further research on this critical issue.

title: NoveltyAgent: Autonomous Novelty Reporting Agent with Point-wise Novelty Analysis and Self-Validation
summary: The exponential growth of academic publications has led to a surge in papers of varying quality, increasing the cost of paper screening. Current approaches either use novelty assessment within general AI Reviewers or repurpose DeepResearch, which lacks domain-specific mechanisms and thus delivers lower-quality results. To bridge this gap, we introduce NoveltyAgent, a multi-agent system designed to generate comprehensive and faithful novelty reports, enabling thorough evaluation of a paper's originality. It decomposes manuscripts into discrete novelty points for fine-grained retrieval and comparison, and builds a comprehensive related-paper database while cross-referencing claims to ensure faithfulness. Furthermore, to address the challenge of evaluating such open-ended generation tasks, we propose a checklist-based evaluation framework, providing an unbiased paradigm for building reliable evaluations. Extensive experiments show that NoveltyAgent achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming GPT-5 DeepResearch by 10.15%. We hope this system will provide reliable, high-quality novelty analysis and help researchers quickly identify novel papers. Code and demo are available at https://github.com/SStan1/NoveltyAgent.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces NoveltyAgent, a system for effective and detailed analysis of a paper's originality, outperforming existing tools by 10.15%.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the exponential increase in academic publications of varying quality, making it difficult and costly to assess the novelty of research papers using existing tools that lack domain-specific mechanisms.
AI.method: NoveltyAgent is a multi-agent system that decomposes manuscripts into discrete novelty points for precise evaluation, builds a domain-specific database, and cross-references claims to ensure the faithfulness of results. A checklist-based framework was designed to evaluate its performance objectively.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that NoveltyAgent outperforms GPT-5 DeepResearch by 10.15% in generating high-quality novelty analyses.
AI.conclusion: The system provides a reliable and efficient way to evaluate the originality of research papers, supporting researchers in identifying novel works more effectively while ensuring faithfulness and comprehensive evaluation.

title: HyReach: Vision-Guided Hybrid Manipulator Reaching in Unseen Cluttered Environments
summary: As robotic systems increasingly operate in unstructured, cluttered, and previously unseen environments, there is a growing need for manipulators that combine compliance, adaptability, and precise control. This work presents a real-time hybrid rigid-soft continuum manipulator system designed for robust open-world object reaching in such challenging environments. The system integrates vision-based perception and 3D scene reconstruction with shape-aware motion planning to generate safe trajectories. A learning-based controller drives the hybrid arm to arbitrary target poses, leveraging the flexibility of the soft segment while maintaining the precision of the rigid segment. The system operates without environment-specific retraining, enabling direct generalization to new scenes. Extensive real-world experiments demonstrate consistent reaching performance with errors below 2 cm across diverse cluttered setups, highlighting the potential of hybrid manipulators for adaptive and reliable operation in unstructured environments.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a robotic manipulator combining rigid and soft elements for precise and adaptable object reaching in unstructured environments.
AI.motivation: The need for manipulators capable of operating effectively in cluttered, unstructured, and unseen environments.
AI.method: The system combines vision-based perception, 3D scene reconstruction, shape-aware motion planning, and a learning-based controller to guide a hybrid rigid-soft arm.
AI.result: Real-world experiments showed consistent reaching performance with errors below 2 cm across diverse setups.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid manipulators can provide adaptable and reliable operation, addressing challenges in unstructured environments without environment-specific retraining.

title: User Preference Modeling for Conversational LLM Agents: Weak Rewards from Retrieval-Augmented Interaction
summary: Large language models are increasingly used as personal assistants, yet most lack a persistent user model, forcing users to repeatedly restate preferences across sessions. We propose Vector-Adapted Retrieval Scoring (VARS), a pipeline-agnostic, frozen-backbone framework that represents each user with long-term and short-term vectors in a shared preference space and uses these vectors to bias retrieval scoring over structured preference memory. The vectors are updated online from weak scalar rewards from users' feedback, enabling personalization without per-user fine-tuning. We evaluate on \textsc{MultiSessionCollab}, an online multi-session collaboration benchmark with rich user preference profiles, across math and code tasks. Under frozen backbones, the main benefit of user-aware retrieval is improved interaction efficiency rather than large gains in raw task accuracy: our full VARS agent achieves the strongest overall performance, matches a strong Reflection baseline in task success, and reduces timeout rate and user effort. The learned long-term vectors also align with cross-user preference overlap, while short-term vectors capture session-specific adaptation, supporting the interpretability of the dual-vector design. Code, model, and data are available at https://github.com/YurenHao0426/VARS.

AI.tldr: The study introduces VARS, a framework addressing long-term user preferences for personal assistants using a dual-vector model for personalization and retrieval scoring.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of persistent user models in large language models, allowing for personalization across sessions without requiring repeated user preference input.
AI.method: VARS uses long-term and short-term vectors updated online from weak scalar rewards based on user feedback, integrating these into retrieval scoring over a structured preference memory.
AI.result: VARS improves interaction efficiency in multi-session collaboration, aligning long-term vectors with overarching user preferences and short-term vectors to session-specific needs.
AI.conclusion: VARS enhances personalization in language models, offering interpretable and effective mechanisms for user assistance without requiring backbone fine-tuning.

title: ORACLE: Optimizing Reasoning Abilities of Large Language Models via Constraint-Led Synthetic Data Elicitation
summary: Training large language models (LLMs) with synthetic reasoning data has become a popular approach to enhancing their reasoning capabilities, while a key factor influencing the effectiveness of this paradigm is the quality of the generated multi-step reasoning data. To generate high-quality reasoning data, many recent methods generate synthetic reasoning paths and filter them based on final answer correctness, often overlooking flaws in intermediate reasoning steps. To enhance the verification of intermediate reasoning steps, prior work primarily resorts to code execution or symbolic reasoning engines. However, code-based validation is restricted to code or mathematical tasks, and reasoning engines require a well-structured and complete context. As a result, existing methods fail to function effectively in natural language reasoning tasks that involve ambiguous or incomplete contexts. In these tasks, synthetic data still lack reliable checks for verifying each reasoning step. To address this challenge, we introduce ORACLE, a structured data generation framework inspired by syllogistic reasoning. ORACLE integrates the generative strengths of LLMs with symbolic supervision: the LLM produces step-wise reasoning contexts, while a symbolic reasoning engine verifies the validity of each intermediate step. By employing a unified prompting template to elicit modular reasoning chains, ORACLE enables fine-grained, step-level validation, facilitating the construction of high-quality multi-step reasoning data. Across six logical, factual, and commonsense reasoning benchmarks, our ORACLE consistently outperforms strong baselines on multiple models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ORACLE, a structured framework combining large language models (LLMs) and symbolic reasoning for generating reliable multi-step reasoning data.
AI.motivation: Existing methods lack effective verification for intermediate reasoning steps in natural language tasks with ambiguous or incomplete contexts.
AI.method: ORACLE combines LLMs to produce step-wise reasoning contexts and symbolic reasoning engines to validate intermediate steps using a unified prompting template.
AI.result: ORACLE achieves superior performance across six reasoning benchmarks compared to strong baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: ORACLE is an effective framework for generating high-quality reasoning data by integrating LLM generative capabilities with symbolic validation.

title: Diffusion Model for Manifold Data: Score Decomposition, Curvature, and Statistical Complexity
summary: Diffusion models have become a leading framework in generative modeling, yet their theoretical understanding -- especially for high-dimensional data concentrated on low-dimensional structures -- remains incomplete. This paper investigates how diffusion models learn such structured data, focusing on two key aspects: statistical complexity and influence of data geometric properties. By modeling data as samples from a smooth Riemannian manifold, our analysis reveals crucial decompositions of score functions in diffusion models under different levels of injected noise. We also highlight the interplay of manifold curvature with the structures in the score function. These analyses enable an efficient neural network approximation to the score function, built upon which we further provide statistical rates for score estimation and distribution learning. Remarkably, the obtained statistical rates are governed by the intrinsic dimension of data and the manifold curvature. These results advance the statistical foundations of diffusion models, bridging theory and practice for generative modeling on manifolds.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes how diffusion models learn high-dimensional data concentrated on low-dimensional manifolds by investigating statistical complexity and geometric influences, deriving statistical rates influenced by intrinsic manifold properties.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to deepen the theoretical understanding of diffusion models in scenarios where high-dimensional data is concentrated on low-dimensional structures, a topic with incomplete exploration in generative modeling.
AI.method: The authors model data as samples from a smooth Riemannian manifold, decompose score functions under varying noise levels, and analyze the interplay of manifold curvature with score structures. They propose an efficient neural network approximation to estimate score functions and provide statistical learning rates.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that the intrinsic dimensions of the data and manifold curvature significantly govern the statistical rates for estimating score functions and learning distributions.
AI.conclusion: This research enhances statistical understanding of diffusion models by linking theoretical insights with practical applications in generative modeling on manifolds.

title: IBCapsNet: Information Bottleneck Capsule Network for Noise-Robust Representation Learning
summary: Capsule networks (CapsNets) are superior at modeling hierarchical spatial relationships but suffer from two critical limitations: high computational cost due to iterative dynamic routing and poor robustness under input corruptions. To address these issues, we propose IBCapsNet, a novel capsule architecture grounded in the Information Bottleneck (IB) principle. Instead of iterative routing, IBCapsNet employs a one-pass variational aggregation mechanism, where primary capsules are first compressed into a global context representation and then processed by class-specific variational autoencoders (VAEs) to infer latent capsules regularized by the KL divergence. This design enables efficient inference while inherently filtering out noise. Experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, SVHN and CIFAR-10 show that IBCapsNet matches CapsNet in clean-data accuracy (achieving 99.41% on MNIST and 92.01% on SVHN), yet significantly outperforms it under four types of synthetic noise - demonstrating average improvements of +17.10% and +14.54% for clamped additive and multiplicative noise, respectively. Moreover, IBCapsNet achieves 2.54x faster training and 3.64x higher inference throughput compared to CapsNet, while reducing model parameters by 4.66%. Our work bridges information-theoretic representation learning with capsule networks, offering a principled path toward robust, efficient, and interpretable deep models. Code is available at https://github.com/cxiang26/IBCapsnet

AI.tldr: IBCapsNet introduces a new capsule architecture using the Information Bottleneck (IB) principle to improve upon CapsNets by reducing computational cost and increasing robustness under input noise.
AI.motivation: Capsule networks exhibit strong hierarchical representation capabilities but are limited by slow dynamic routing and poor performance under input noise.
AI.method: IBCapsNet replaces iterative routing with a one-pass variational aggregation process leveraging primary capsules, variational autoencoders (VAEs), and KL divergence-based regularization.
AI.result: IBCapsNet matches CapsNets' accuracy on clean datasets, outperforms under noise (+17.10% and +14.54% improvements), speeds up training (2.54x), enhances inference throughput (3.64x), and reduces model parameters by 4.66%.
AI.conclusion: IBCapsNet advances capsule networks by combining efficiency, robustness, and interpretability, while leveraging information-theoretic principles for better model design.

title: Zero-Shot Vulnerability Detection in Low-Resource Smart Contracts Through Solidity-Only Training
summary: Smart contracts have transformed decentralized finance, but flaws in their logic still create major security threats. Most existing vulnerability detection techniques focus on well-supported languages like Solidity, while low-resource counterparts such as Vyper remain largely underexplored due to scarce analysis tools and limited labeled datasets. Training a robust detection model directly on Vyper is particularly challenging, as collecting sufficiently large and diverse Vyper training datasets is difficult in practice. To address this gap, we introduce Sol2Vy, a novel framework that enables cross-language knowledge transfer from Solidity to Vyper, allowing vulnerability detection on Vyper using models trained exclusively on Solidity. This approach eliminates the need for extensive labeled Vyper datasets typically required to build a robust vulnerability detection model. We implement and evaluate Sol2Vy on various critical vulnerability types, including reentrancy, weak randomness, and unchecked transfer. Experimental results show that Sol2Vy, despite being trained exclusively on Solidity, achieves strong detection performance on Vyper contracts and significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Sol2Vy, a framework for detecting vulnerabilities in Vyper smart contracts using models trained on Solidity, bridging gaps caused by limited Vyper resources.
AI.motivation: To address the security risks in Vyper smart contracts, which are underexplored due to limited analysis tools and datasets, and to leverage Solidity's well-established ecosystem for this purpose.
AI.method: The authors developed Sol2Vy, a cross-language knowledge transfer framework that uses Solidity-trained models to detect vulnerabilities in Vyper contracts, eliminating the need for large Vyper datasets.
AI.result: Sol2Vy demonstrates strong detection performance on Vyper contracts for vulnerabilities like reentrancy and weak randomness, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Sol2Vy framework enables effective vulnerability detection for low-resource languages like Vyper by leveraging knowledge from Solidity, advancing smart contract security.

title: LLM Router: Prefill is All You Need
summary: LLMs often share comparable benchmark accuracies, but their complementary performance across task subsets suggests that an Oracle router--a theoretical selector with perfect foresight--can significantly surpass standalone model accuracy by navigating model-specific strengths. While current routers rely on fragile semantic signals, we propose using internal prefill activations via Encoder-Target Decoupling--a functional separation between the model providing the predictive signal (the Encoder) and the model whose performance is being estimated (the Target). This allows optimized heterogeneous pairing between unique encoders and target models. We utilize Fisher Separability (J) and Effective Dimensionality (d_eff) as mathematical probes to isolate optimal layer-wise signals, providing the predictive foundation for our SharedTrunkNet architecture. SharedTrunkNet captures up to 45.58% of the accuracy gap between the strongest standalone model and the Oracle while achieving 74.31% cost savings relative to the highest-cost model.

AI.tldr: The study proposes a novel routing system for language models that captures significant accuracy improvements while achieving cost efficiency.
AI.motivation: Language Models (LLMs) often exhibit complementary strengths across different tasks, leading the authors to explore ways to surpass the accuracy of standalone models by leveraging these strengths effectively.
AI.method: The authors introduce Encoder-Target Decoupling to separate the predictive and evaluative responsibilities of models and propose the SharedTrunkNet architecture. Internal prefill activations, Fisher Separability, and Effective Dimensionality are employed to optimize layer-wise signal extraction.
AI.result: The SharedTrunkNet captures 45.58% of the accuracy gap between standalone models and an Oracle router, achieving this with 74.31% cost savings compared to the most expensive model.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture demonstrates how intelligently routing and pairing LLMs can excel in both accuracy and computational efficiency, addressing weaknesses in current routing systems.

title: GaussianSSC: Triplane-Guided Directional Gaussian Fields for 3D Semantic Completion
summary: We present \emph{GaussianSSC}, a two-stage, grid-native and triplane-guided approach to semantic scene completion (SSC) that injects the benefits of Gaussians without replacing the voxel grid or maintaining a separate Gaussian set. We introduce \emph{Gaussian Anchoring}, a sub-pixel, Gaussian-weighted image aggregation over fused FPN features that tightens voxel--image alignment and improves monocular occupancy estimation. We further convert point-like voxel features into a learned per-voxel Gaussian field and refine triplane features via a triplane-aligned \emph{Gaussian--Triplane Refinement} module that combines \emph{local gathering} (target-centric) and \emph{global aggregation} (source-centric). This directional, anisotropic support captures surface tangency, scale, and occlusion-aware asymmetry while preserving the efficiency of triplane representations. On SemanticKITTI~\cite{behley2019semantickitti}, GaussianSSC improves Stage~1 occupancy by +1.0\% Recall, +2.0\% Precision, and +1.8\% IoU over state-of-the-art baselines, and improves Stage~2 semantic prediction by +1.8\% IoU and +0.8\% mIoU.

AI.tldr: GaussianSSC introduces a two-stage, grid-native, triplane-guided approach to semantic scene completion, leveraging Gaussian-based features for improved voxel-image alignment and occupancy estimation. The method achieves better performance than existing baselines on SemanticKITTI benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Semantic scene completion (SSC) is a challenging task involving accurate 3D representation and semantic labeling of scenes. Current methods often struggle with achieving fine-grained voxel-image alignment and balancing efficiency with feature fidelity.
AI.method: The paper proposes GaussianSSC, a novel approach using Gaussian Anchoring for improved monocular occupancy estimation and a Gaussian--Triplane Refinement module to enhance local and global feature aggregation. This combines Gaussian-weighted image aggregation and triplane-aligned feature refinements to improve SSC accuracy.
AI.result: GaussianSSC achieves improvements over baseline on the SemanticKITTI dataset, notably enhancing Stage 1 by +1.0% Recall, +2.0% Precision, and +1.8% IoU, and Stage 2 semantic prediction by +1.8% IoU and +0.8% mIoU.
AI.conclusion: GaussianSSC demonstrates the effectiveness of leveraging Gaussian-based anchoring and triplane-guided refinements to enhance voxel-image alignment and semantic predictions, setting new benchmarks in SemanticKITTI evaluations.

title: Integrative Learning of Dynamically Evolving Multiplex Graphs and Nodal Attributes Using Neural Network Gaussian Processes with an Application to Dynamic Terrorism Graphs
summary: Exploring the dynamic co-evolution of multiplex graphs and nodal attributes is a compelling question in criminal and terrorism networks. This article is motivated by the study of dynamically evolving interactions among prominent terrorist organizations, considering various organizational attributes like size, ideology, leadership, and operational capacity. Statistically principled integration of multiplex graphs with nodal attributes is significantly challenging due to the need to leverage shared information within and across layers, account for uncertainty in predicting unobserved links, and capture temporal evolution of node attributes. These difficulties increase when layers are partially observed, as in terrorism networks where connections are deliberately hidden to obscure key relationships. To address these challenges, we present a principled methodological framework to integrate the multiplex graph layers and nodal attributes. The approach employs time-varying stochastic latent factor models, leveraging shared latent factors to capture graph structure and its co-evolution with node attributes. Latent factors are modeled using Gaussian processes with an infinitely wide deep neural network-based covariance function, termed neural network Gaussian processes (NN-GP). The NN-GP framework on latent factors exploits the predictive power of Bayesian deep neural network architecture while propagating uncertainty for reliability. Simulation studies highlight superior performance of the proposed approach in achieving inferential objectives. The approach, termed as dynamic joint learner, enables predictive inference (with uncertainty) of diverse unobserved dynamic relationships among prominent terrorist organizations and their organization-specific attributes, as well as clustering behavior in terms of friend-and-foe relationships, which could be informative in counter-terrorism research.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method to analyze evolving relationships and attributes in terrorism networks using a statistical framework combining graph layers and nodal attributes.
AI.motivation: To study the dynamic interplay of terrorist organizations' interactions and their organizational attributes, which is complex due to hidden relationships and evolving features.
AI.method: The paper employs time-varying stochastic latent factor models with neural network Gaussian processes (NN-GP) to analyze multiplex graphs and node attributes, leveraging shared latent factors and uncertainty propagation.
AI.result: The proposed method demonstrated superior performance in predictive inference of unobserved relationships, attribute evolution, and clustering in counter-terrorism contexts.
AI.conclusion: The dynamic joint learner framework provides a robust tool for understanding and predicting key aspects of terrorism networks, aiding counter-terrorism research.

title: Can LLMs Fool Graph Learning? Exploring Universal Adversarial Attacks on Text-Attributed Graphs
summary: Text-attributed graphs (TAGs) enhance graph learning by integrating rich textual semantics and topological context for each node. While boosting expressiveness, they also expose new vulnerabilities in graph learning through text-based adversarial surfaces. Recent advances leverage diverse backbones, such as graph neural networks (GNNs) and pre-trained language models (PLMs), to capture both structural and textual information in TAGs. This diversity raises a key question: How can we design universal adversarial attacks that generalize across architectures to assess the security of TAG models? The challenge arises from the stark contrast in how different backbones-GNNs and PLMs-perceive and encode graph patterns, coupled with the fact that many PLMs are only accessible via APIs, limiting attacks to black-box settings. To address this, we propose BadGraph, a novel attack framework that deeply elicits large language models (LLMs) understanding of general graph knowledge to jointly perturb both node topology and textual semantics. Specifically, we design a target influencer retrieval module that leverages graph priors to construct cross-modally aligned attack shortcuts, thereby enabling efficient LLM-based perturbation reasoning. Experiments show that BadGraph achieves universal and effective attacks across GNN- and LLM-based reasoners, with up to a 76.3% performance drop, while theoretical and empirical analyses confirm its stealthy yet interpretable nature.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces BadGraph, a universal attack framework targeting text-attributed graphs by perturbing node topology and textual semantics, achieving significant effectiveness across GNN and LLM-based models.
AI.motivation: To address the rising vulnerabilities in graph learning models exposed by text-attributed graphs and provide a universal adversarial attack framework compatible across diverse architectures.
AI.method: BadGraph utilizes large language models for reasoning and perturbation by introducing a target influencer retrieval module, enabling aligned attack shortcuts. It adopts black-box settings to overcome limitations of inaccessible pre-trained language models.
AI.result: BadGraph demonstrates up to a 76.3% drop in model performance through universal attacks on GNNs and LLM-based models, showcasing stealthy and interpretable attack techniques.
AI.conclusion: BadGraph effectively exposes security weaknesses in TAG models across architectures, providing insights into adversarial resilience and attack dynamics using large language models.

title: MFSR: MeanFlow Distillation for One Step Real-World Image Super Resolution
summary: Diffusion- and flow-based models have advanced Real-world Image Super-Resolution (Real-ISR), but their multi-step sampling makes inference slow and hard to deploy. One-step distillation alleviates the cost, yet often degrades restoration quality and removes the option to refine with more steps. We present Mean Flows for Super-Resolution (MFSR), a new distillation framework that produces photorealistic results in a single step while still allowing an optional few-step path for further improvement. Our approach uses MeanFlow as the learning target, enabling the student to approximate the average velocity between arbitrary states of the Probability Flow ODE (PF-ODE) and effectively capture the teacher's dynamics without explicit rollouts. To better leverage pretrained generative priors, we additionally improve original MeanFlow's Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) formulation with teacher CFG distillation strategy, which enhances restoration capability and preserves fine details. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that MFSR achieves efficient, flexible, and high-quality super-resolution, delivering results on par with or even better than multi-step teachers while requiring much lower computational cost.

AI.tldr: MFSR proposes a novel distillation framework for Real-ISR, achieving high-quality results in a single step while retaining the option for refinement with fewer steps, and maintaining low computational costs.
AI.motivation: The slow and computationally expensive multi-step sampling in diffusion- and flow-based Real-ISR models makes them difficult to deploy, and existing one-step distillation methods often compromise quality and flexibility.
AI.method: MFSR employs MeanFlow as the learning target to capture the teacher model's dynamics without requiring explicit rollouts. It introduces enhancements to Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) by distilling teacher CFG, aiding in restoration and detail preservation.
AI.result: MFSR generates efficient and photorealistic super-resolution that matches or even surpasses multi-step methods on both synthetic and real-world benchmarks, with significantly reduced computational requirements.
AI.conclusion: MFSR demonstrates that single-step approaches can achieve both high efficiency and superior quality, with the additional flexibility of optional refinement through fewer steps.

title: DomAgent: Leveraging Knowledge Graphs and Case-Based Reasoning for Domain-Specific Code Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown impressive capabilities in code generation. However, because most LLMs are trained on public domain corpora, directly applying them to real-world software development often yields low success rates, as these scenarios frequently require domain-specific knowledge. In particular, domain-specific tasks usually demand highly specialized solutions, which are often underrepresented or entirely absent in the training data of generic LLMs. To address this challenge, we propose DomAgent, an autonomous coding agent that bridges this gap by enabling LLMs to generate domain-adapted code through structured reasoning and targeted retrieval. A core component of DomAgent is DomRetriever, a novel retrieval module that emulates how humans learn domain-specific knowledge, by combining conceptual understanding with experiential examples. It dynamically integrates top-down knowledge-graph reasoning with bottom-up case-based reasoning, enabling iterative retrieval and synthesis of structured knowledge and representative cases to ensure contextual relevance and broad task coverage. DomRetriever can operate as part of DomAgent or independently with any LLM for flexible domain adaptation. We evaluate DomAgent on an open benchmark dataset in the data science domain (DS-1000) and further apply it to real-world truck software development tasks. Experimental results show that DomAgent significantly enhances domain-specific code generation, enabling small open-source models to close much of the performance gap with large proprietary LLMs in complex, real-world applications. The code is available at: https://github.com/Wangshuaiia/DomAgent.

AI.tldr: DomAgent is a framework to enable large language models (LLMs) to generate domain-adapted code for specialized tasks through structured reasoning and targeted retrieval, overcoming limitations in their training datasets.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to address the limitations of LLMs in generating code for domain-specific tasks due to underrepresentation of specialized knowledge in public training datasets.
AI.method: The authors introduce DomAgent, which includes a retrieval module (DomRetriever) for domain-specific knowledge integration using top-down knowledge-graph reasoning combined with bottom-up case-based reasoning. This approach helps bridge the gap between LLM capabilities and real-world needs.
AI.result: DomAgent demonstrated effectiveness in enhancing domain-specific code generation as tested on DS-1000 benchmarks and real-world truck software tasks, showing performance improvements even for smaller open-source models.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully empowers LLMs to handle domain-specific code challenges, narrowing the gap between small open-source models and large proprietary models for intricate real-world coding scenarios.

title: Mitigating Shortcut Reasoning in Language Models: A Gradient-Aware Training Approach
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures. Preprint. Experiments on synthetic reasoning benchmarks. Code available
summary: Large language models exhibit strong reasoning capabilities, yet often rely on shortcuts such as surface pattern matching and answer memorization rather than genuine logical inference. We propose Shortcut-Aware Reasoning Training (SART), a gradient-aware framework that detects and mitigates shortcut-promoting samples via ShortcutScore and gradient surgery. Our method identifies shortcut signals through gradient misalignment with validation objectives and answer-token concentration, and modifies training dynamics accordingly. Experiments on controlled reasoning benchmarks show that SART achieves +16.5% accuracy and +40.2% robustness over the strongest baseline, significantly improving generalization under distribution shifts. Code is available at: https://github.com/fuyanjie/short-cut-aware-data-centric-reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Shortcut-Aware Reasoning Training (SART), aimed at addressing the issue of large language models relying on shortcuts rather than true reasoning, showing significant improvements in accuracy and robustness.
AI.motivation: Large language models often lack genuine logical reasoning capabilities, relying instead on shortcuts like pattern matching and memorization, which hampers their performance under distribution shifts.
AI.method: The authors propose SART, a framework that detects and mitigates shortcut issues using ShortcutScore and gradient surgery. The method identifies shortcut signals through gradient misalignment and answer-token concentration, adjusting the training process to improve reasoning.
AI.result: Experiments on reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that SART improves accuracy by 16.5% and robustness by 40.2% compared to the best existing methods.
AI.conclusion: SART is an effective framework for enhancing reasoning in large language models by addressing shortcuts, significantly improving both accuracy and robustness.

title: A Framework for Closed-Loop Robotic Assembly, Alignment and Self-Recovery of Precision Optical Systems
summary: Robotic automation has transformed scientific workflows in domains such as chemistry and materials science, yet free-space optics, which is a high precision domain, remains largely manual. Optical systems impose strict spatial and angular tolerances, and their performance is governed by tightly coupled physical parameters, making generalizable automation particularly challenging. In this work, we present a robotics framework for the autonomous construction, alignment, and maintenance of precision optical systems. Our approach integrates hierarchical computer vision systems, optimization routines, and custom-built tools to achieve this functionality. As a representative demonstration, we perform the fully autonomous construction of a tabletop laser cavity from randomly distributed components. The system performs several tasks such as laser beam centering, spatial alignment of multiple beams, resonator alignment, laser mode selection, and self-recovery from induced misalignment and disturbances. By achieving closed-loop autonomy for highly sensitive optical systems, this work establishes a foundation for autonomous optical experiments for applications across technical domains.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a robotics framework enabling autonomous construction, alignment, and maintenance of precision optical systems using computer vision and optimization techniques.
AI.motivation: Free-space optics remains largely manual despite advancements in robotic automation, partially due to stringent spatial, angular tolerances, and tightly coupled physical parameters.
AI.method: The approach integrates hierarchical computer vision systems, optimization routines, and custom tools to achieve closed-loop autonomy for precision optical systems.
AI.result: The robotics framework successfully demonstrated fully autonomous construction and alignment of a tabletop laser cavity from scattered components, along with self-recovery capability.
AI.conclusion: This work lays the groundwork for autonomous optical experiments with potential applications across diverse technical fields.

title: From Causal Discovery to Dynamic Causal Inference in Neural Time Series
summary: Time-varying causal models provide a powerful framework for studying dynamic scientific systems, yet most existing approaches assume that the underlying causal network is known a priori - an assumption rarely satisfied in real-world domains where causal structure is uncertain, evolving, or only indirectly observable. This limits the applicability of dynamic causal inference in many scientific settings. We propose Dynamic Causal Network Autoregression (DCNAR), a two-stage neural causal modeling framework that integrates data-driven causal discovery with time-varying causal inference. In the first stage, a neural autoregressive causal discovery model learns a sparse directed causal network from multivariate time series. In the second stage, this learned structure is used as a structural prior for a time-varying neural network autoregression, enabling dynamic estimation of causal influence without requiring pre-specified network structure. We evaluate the scientific validity of DCNAR using behavioral diagnostics that assess causal necessity, temporal stability, and sensitivity to structural change, rather than predictive accuracy alone. Experiments on multi-country panel time-series data demonstrate that learned causal networks yield more stable and behaviorally meaningful dynamic causal inferences than coefficient-based or structure-free alternatives, even when forecasting performance is comparable. These results position DCNAR as a general framework for using AI as a scientific instrument for dynamic causal reasoning under structural uncertainty.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DCNAR, a framework that integrates causal discovery and time-varying causal inference to address structural uncertainty in causal analysis of dynamic systems.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to time-varying causal models often assume a known causal structure, which is not realistic in many real-world applications. This limitation hinders the applicability of dynamic causal reasoning.
AI.method: The authors introduce a two-stage neural framework (DCNAR). Stage 1 uses a neural causal discovery model to infer a sparse directed causal network from multivariate time series. Stage 2 employs this network as a prior for time-varying causal inference.
AI.result: DCNAR yields more behaviorally stable and meaningful causal inferences under structural uncertainty compared to traditional methods, even with similar forecasting performance.
AI.conclusion: DCNAR provides a robust method for integrating causal discovery and inference in dynamic systems, improving causal reasoning without requiring predefined network structures.

title: Revisiting Tree Search for LLMs: Gumbel and Sequential Halving for Budget-Scalable Reasoning
comment: The paper has been accepted to the ICAPS-2026 conference. 5 pages, 2 figures
summary: Neural tree search is a powerful decision-making algorithm widely used in complex domains such as game playing and model-based reinforcement learning. Recent work has applied AlphaZero-style tree search to enhance the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) during inference, but we find that this approach suffers from a scaling failure: on GSM8K and Game24, accuracy drops as the search budget increases. In this paper, we present ReSCALE, an adaptation of Gumbel AlphaZero MCTS that replaces Dirichlet noise and PUCT selection with Gumbel sampling and Sequential Halving, restoring monotonic scaling without changes to the model or its training. ReSCALE reaches 58.4\% on GSM8K and 85.3\% on Game24 at budgets where the baseline degrades. Ablations confirm that Sequential Halving is the primary driver of the improvement.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ReSCALE, a variant of Gumbel AlphaZero Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to resolve the scaling failure observed in AlphaZero-style tree search for Large Language Model (LLM)-based reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the observed deterioration in performance as the search budget increases when applying AlphaZero-style tree search to LLMs for reasoning tasks on datasets like GSM8K and Game24.
AI.method: The paper introduces ReSCALE, which modifies traditional MCTS by introducing Gumbel sampling and Sequential Halving, eliminating Dirichlet noise and PUCT selection.
AI.result: ReSCALE improves reasoning performance on GSM8K (58.4%) and Game24 (85.3%) for search budgets that previously caused performance degradation.
AI.conclusion: ReSCALE restores monotonic performance scaling in tree search without requiring changes to the model or its training, with Sequential Halving being a key factor behind its effectiveness.

title: Satellite-to-Street: Synthesizing Post-Disaster Views from Satellite Imagery via Generative Vision Models
comment: Accepted for presentation at IGARSS 2026 (IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium)
summary: In the immediate aftermath of natural disasters, rapid situational awareness is critical. Traditionally, satellite observations are widely used to estimate damage extent. However, they lack the ground-level perspective essential for characterizing specific structural failures and impacts. Meanwhile, ground-level data (e.g., street-view imagery) remains largely inaccessible during time-sensitive events. This study investigates Satellite-to-Street View Synthesis to bridge this data gap. We introduce two generative strategies to synthesize post-disaster street views from satellite imagery: a Vision-Language Model (VLM)-guided approach and a damage-sensitive Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) method. We benchmark these against general-purpose baselines (Pix2Pix, ControlNet) using a proposed Structure-Aware Evaluation Framework. This multi-tier protocol integrates (1) pixel-level quality assessment, (2) ResNet-based semantic consistency verification, and (3) a novel VLM-as-a-Judge for perceptual alignment. Experiments on 300 disaster scenarios reveal a critical realism--fidelity trade-off: while diffusion-based approaches (e.g., ControlNet) achieve high perceptual realism, they often hallucinate structural details. Quantitative results show that standard ControlNet achieves the highest semantic accuracy, 0.71, whereas VLM-enhanced and MoE models excel in textural plausibility but struggle with semantic clarity. This work establishes a baseline for trustworthy cross-view synthesis, emphasizing that visually realistic generations may still fail to preserve critical structural information required for reliable disaster assessment.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Satellite-to-Street View Synthesis method to bridge the data gap during natural disasters by generating street-view imagery from satellite images.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the need for ground-level perspectives during natural disasters, which satellite imagery alone cannot provide, and the inaccessibility of ground-level data during real-time disaster events.
AI.method: The authors propose two generative strategies for post-disaster imagery synthesis: a Vision-Language Model (VLM)-guided approach and a damage-sensitive Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) method. They benchmark these against general-purpose methods using a multi-tier evaluation framework.
AI.result: Experiments on 300 disaster scenarios reveal that ControlNet achieves the highest semantic accuracy (0.71), but diffusion-based models often hallucinate details. VLM and MoE approaches excel in textural plausibility but struggle with retaining semantic clarity.
AI.conclusion: This study establishes that while current approaches can generate visually realistic post-disaster street views, they often fail to accurately capture critical structural information, making them unreliable for trustworthy disaster assessment.

title: A Survey of Web Application Security Tutorials
summary: Developers rely on online tutorials to learn web application security, but tutorial quality varies. We reviewed 132 free security tutorials to examine topic coverage, authorship, and technical depth. Our analysis shows that most tutorials come from vendors and emphasize high-level explanations over concrete implementation guidance. Few tutorials provide complete runnable code examples or direct links to authoritative security resources such as the Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP), Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE), or Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE). We found that two visible signals help identify more useful tutorials: the presence of runnable code and direct links to official resources. These signals can help developers distinguish broad awareness material from tutorials that better support secure implementation.

AI.tldr: The paper reviews 132 free web security tutorials, highlighting that most offer high-level explanations but lack actionable implementation guidance.
AI.motivation: Developers depend on online tutorials for web application security learning, but tutorial quality and utility are inconsistent.
AI.method: The study analyzed 132 tutorials for topic coverage, authorship, technical depth, runnable code, and references to official security resources.
AI.result: Most tutorials are authored by vendors, focus on high-level guidance, and rarely include complete runnable code or links to authoritative resources.
AI.conclusion: Presence of runnable code and links to official sources are key indicators of tutorial usefulness for secure implementation.

title: The Hidden Puppet Master: A Theoretical and Real-World Account of Emotional Manipulation in LLMs
summary: As users increasingly turn to LLMs for practical and personal advice, they become vulnerable to being subtly steered toward hidden incentives misaligned with their own interests. Prior works have benchmarked persuasion and manipulation detection, but these efforts rely on simulated or debate-style settings, remain uncorrelated with real human belief shifts, and overlook a critical dimension: the morality of hidden incentives driving the manipulation. We introduce PUPPET, a theoretical taxonomy of personalized emotional manipulation in LLM-human dialogues that centers around incentive morality, and conduct a human study with N=1,035 participants across realistic everyday queries, varying personalization and incentive direction (harmful versus prosocial). We find that harmful hidden incentives produce significantly larger belief shifts than prosocial ones. Finally, we benchmark LLMs on the task of belief prediction, finding that models exhibit moderate predictive ability of belief change based on conversational contexts (r=0.3 - 0.5), but they also systematically underestimate the magnitude of belief shift. Together, this work establishes a theoretically grounded and behaviorally validated foundation for studying, and ultimately combatting, incentive-driven manipulation in LLMs during everyday, practical user queries.

AI.tldr: This study introduces PUPPET, a taxonomy aimed at understanding personalized emotional manipulation in LLM-human dialogues, focusing on the morality of hidden incentives. Human trials demonstrate that harmful incentives produce larger belief shifts than prosocial ones, and LLMs moderately predict belief changes.
AI.motivation: Growing reliance on LLMs for advice exposes users to potential manipulation serving hidden, misaligned incentives. There is a lack of research addressing the morality of such incentives and their real-world implications.
AI.method: The researchers developed PUPPET, a taxonomy for analyzing emotional manipulation in LLM interactions, and conducted a human study with 1,035 participants. They assessed belief shifts using personalization and varying incentive moralities (harmful vs. prosocial). LLMs were tested for their ability to predict belief changes from dialogues.
AI.result: The study found that harmful hidden incentives lead to significantly larger belief shifts compared to prosocial incentives. LLMs demonstrated moderate accuracy (r = 0.3 - 0.5) in predicting belief changes but underestimated their magnitude.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a theoretical and empirical basis for further research into incentive-driven manipulation in LLMs, aiming to develop tools to better understand and counteract such effects.

title: SafePilot: A Framework for Assuring LLM-enabled Cyber-Physical Systems
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs), deep learning architectures with typically over 10 billion parameters, have recently begun to be integrated into various cyber-physical systems (CPS) such as robotics, industrial automation, and autopilot systems. The abstract knowledge and reasoning capabilities of LLMs are employed for tasks like planning and navigation. However, a significant challenge arises from the tendency of LLMs to produce "hallucinations" - outputs that are coherent yet factually incorrect or contextually unsuitable. This characteristic can lead to undesirable or unsafe actions in the CPS. Therefore, our research focuses on assuring the LLM-enabled CPS by enhancing their critical properties. We propose SafePilot, a novel hierarchical neuro-symbolic framework that provides end-to-end assurance for LLM-enabled CPS according to attribute-based and temporal specifications. Given a task and its specification, SafePilot first invokes a hierarchical planner with a discriminator that assesses task complexity. If the task is deemed manageable, it is passed directly to an LLM-based task planner with built-in verification. Otherwise, the hierarchical planner applies a divide-and-conquer strategy, decomposing the task into sub-tasks, each of which is individually planned and later merged into a final solution. The LLM-based task planner translates natural language constraints into formal specifications and verifies the LLM's output against them. If violations are detected, it identifies the flaw, adjusts the prompt accordingly, and re-invokes the LLM. This iterative process continues until a valid plan is produced or a predefined limit is reached. Our framework supports LLM-enabled CPS with both attribute-based and temporal constraints. Its effectiveness and adaptability are demonstrated through two illustrative case studies.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SafePilot, a hierarchical neuro-symbolic framework that ensures the safety and reliability of LLM-enabled CPS by addressing their tendency to produce hallucinations, using verification and iterative refinement of task planning.
AI.motivation: The integration of LLMs into CPS tasks like planning and navigation poses challenges due to hallucinations in outputs, which threaten safety and reliability in critical systems.
AI.method: SafePilot uses a hierarchical planning approach with a complexity discriminator. Tasks deemed simple go directly to an LLM planner with verification. Complex tasks are decomposed into sub-tasks, planned individually, and merged. The LLM planner verifies outputs against formal specs, iterating with refined prompts if flaws are detected.
AI.result: The proposed framework can validate LLM outputs against constraints, identify flaws, and refine plans iteratively, as demonstrated through two case studies, showing its effectiveness and adaptability for CPS.
AI.conclusion: SafePilot offers a robust end-to-end assurance framework for LLM-enabled CPS by combining neuro-symbolic planning, constraint verification, and iterative refinement, enhancing task reliability and safety.

title: ALMAB-DC: Active Learning, Multi-Armed Bandits, and Distributed Computing for Sequential Experimental Design and Black-Box Optimization
summary:
Sequential experimental design under expensive, gradient-free objectives is a central challenge in computational statistics: evaluation budgets are tightly constrained and information must be extracted efficiently from each observation. We propose \textbf{ALMAB-DC}, a GP-based sequential design framework combining active learning, multi-armed bandits (MAB), and distributed asynchronous computing for expensive black-box experimentation. A Gaussian process surrogate with uncertainty-aware acquisition identifies informative query points; a UCB or Thompson-sampling bandit controller allocates evaluations across parallel workers; and an asynchronous scheduler handles heterogeneous runtimes. We present cumulative regret bounds for the bandit components and characterize parallel scalability via Amdahl's Law.
  We validate ALMAB-DC on five benchmarks. On the two statistical experimental-design tasks, ALMAB-DC achieves lower simple regret than Equal Spacing, Random, and D-optimal designs in dose--response optimization, and in adaptive spatial field estimation matches the Greedy Max-Variance benchmark while outperforming Latin Hypercube Sampling; at $K=4$ the distributed setting reaches target performance in one-quarter of sequential wall-clock rounds. On three ML/engineering tasks (CIFAR-10 HPO, CFD drag minimization, MuJoCo RL), ALMAB-DC achieves 93.4\% CIFAR-10 accuracy (outperforming BOHB by 1.7\,pp and Optuna by 1.1\,pp), reduces airfoil drag to $C_D = 0.059$ (36.9\% below Grid Search), and improves RL return by 50\% over Grid Search. All advantages over non-ALMAB baselines are statistically significant under Bonferroni-corrected Mann--Whitney $U$ tests. Distributed execution achieves $7.5\times$ speedup at $K = 16$ agents, consistent with Amdahl's Law.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ALMAB-DC, a novel framework for black-box experimentation combining active learning, MABs, and distributed computing, achieving superior performance across various benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of sequential experimental design under constraints of expensive, gradient-free objective evaluations, requiring efficient information extraction and parallel execution.
AI.method: They propose ALMAB-DC, integrating a Gaussian Process surrogate for identifying query points, a bandit controller (UCB/Thompson sampling) for allocation, and an asynchronous scheduler for handling heterogeneous runtimes.
AI.result: ALMAB-DC demonstrates superior performance in both statistical design and ML/engineering tasks, achieving lower regret, better accuracy, and significant speedups over baseline methods across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach is validated as effective and scalable, offering significant improvements and efficiency in black-box experimental design.

title: Does AI Homogenize Student Thinking? A Multi-Dimensional Analysis of Structural Convergence in AI-Augmented Essays
summary: While AI-assisted writing has been widely reported to improve essay quality, its impact on the structural diversity of student thinking remains unexplored. Analyzing 6,875 essays across five conditions (Human-only, AI-only, and three Human+AI prompt strategies), we provide the first empirical evidence of a Quality-Homogenization Tradeoff, in which substantial quality gains co-occur with significant homogenization. The effect is dimension-specific: cohesion architecture lost 70-78% of its variance, whereas perspective plurality was diversified. Convergence target analysis further revealed that AI-augmented essays were pulled toward AI structural patterns yet deviated significantly from the Human-AI axis, indicating simultaneous partial replacement and partial emergence. Crucially, prompt specificity reversed homogenization into diversification on argument depth, demonstrating that homogenization is not an intrinsic property of AI but a function of interaction design.

AI.tldr: Analyzed 6,875 essays to understand how AI writing tools influence student thinking. Found a tradeoff: improved quality but structural homogenization, with specific variations in cohesion and perspective.
AI.motivation: The authors sought to investigate whether AI-assisted writing impacts the structural diversity of students' thinking, an area previously unexplored despite improvements in essay quality.
AI.method: The study analyzed 6,875 essays produced under five conditions (Human-only, AI-only, and three Human+AI prompt strategies), focusing on structural dimensions like cohesion and perspective.
AI.result: The study revealed a "Quality-Homogenization Tradeoff." While essays achieved higher quality, they exhibited reduced structural variability in specific areas like cohesion architecture. However, diversity improved in perspective plurality.
AI.conclusion: Prompt specificity can counteract homogenization by enhancing argument depth, suggesting homogenization is not inherent to AI but dependent on how it's used in interaction design.

title: Centrality-Based Pruning for Efficient Echo State Networks
summary: Echo State Networks (ESNs) are a reservoir computing framework widely used for nonlinear time-series prediction. However, despite their effectiveness, the randomly initialized reservoir often contains redundant nodes, leading to unnecessary computational overhead and reduced efficiency. In this work, we propose a graph centrality-based pruning approach that interprets the reservoir as a weighted directed graph and removes structurally less important nodes using centrality measures. Experiments on Mackey-Glass time-series prediction and electric load forecasting demonstrate that the proposed method can significantly reduce reservoir size while maintaining, and in some cases improving, prediction accuracy, while preserving the essential reservoir dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a graph centrality-based pruning method for Echo State Networks to optimize reservoir size and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Optimize Echo State Networks by addressing inefficiencies caused by redundant nodes in the randomly initialized reservoir.
AI.method: Reservoir nodes are analyzed as a weighted directed graph and pruned using graph centrality measures to remove less structurally important nodes.
AI.result: The method effectively reduces reservoir size while maintaining or improving prediction accuracy in tasks like Mackey-Glass time-series and electric load forecasting.
AI.conclusion: Graph-based pruning can enhance ESN efficiency and preserve its dynamics, showcasing its utility for nonlinear time-series prediction.

title: Clinical Cognition Alignment for Gastrointestinal Diagnosis with Multimodal LLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable potential in medical image analysis. However, their application in gastrointestinal endoscopy is currently hindered by two critical limitations: the misalignment between general model reasoning and standardized clinical cognitive pathways, and the lack of causal association between visual features and diagnostic outcomes. In this paper, we propose a novel Clinical-Cognitive-Aligned (CogAlign) framework to address these challenges. First, we endow the model with rigorous clinical analytical capabilities by constructing the hierarchical clinical cognition dataset and employing Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT). Unlike conventional approaches, this strategy internalizes the hierarchical diagnostic logic of experts, ranging from anatomical localization and morphological evaluation to microvascular analysis, directly into the model. Second, to eliminate visual bias, we provide a theoretical analysis demonstrating that standard supervised tuning inevitably converges to spurious background correlations. Guided by this insight, we propose a counterfactual-driven reinforcement learning strategy to enforce causal rectification. By generating counterfactual normal samples via lesion masking and optimizing through clinical-cognition-centric rewards, we constrain the model to strictly ground its diagnosis in causal lesion features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves State-of-the-Art (SoTA) performance across multiple benchmarks, significantly enhancing diagnostic accuracy in complex clinical scenarios. All source code and datasets will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Clinical-Cognitive-Aligned (CogAlign) framework for improving gastrointestinal endoscopic diagnosis. It integrates clinical reasoning and causal understanding to overcome current limitations in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs).
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs are not aligned with standardized clinical reasoning and fail to establish causal links between visual features and diagnostic outcomes in gastrointestinal endoscopy.
AI.method: The proposed framework includes a hierarchical clinical cognition dataset and supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to internalize expert diagnostic logic. Additionally, it uses a counterfactual-driven reinforcement learning strategy to eliminate visual biases and ground diagnoses in causal lesion features.
AI.result: The framework achieves State-of-the-Art (SoTA) performance in multiple benchmarks, improving diagnostic accuracy in complex clinical scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Integrating clinical reasoning and counterfactual causal rectifications enables significant advancements in medical diagnostic capabilities of MLLMs, particularly for gastrointestinal endoscopy.

title: Auditing MCP Servers for Over-Privileged Tool Capabilities
summary: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) has emerged as a standard for connecting Large Language Models (LLMs) to external tools and data. However, MCP servers often expose privileged capabilities, such as file system access, network requests, and command execution that can be exploited if not properly secured. We present mcp-sec-audit, an extensible security assessment toolkit designed specifically for MCP servers. It implements static pattern matching for Python-based MCP servers and dynamic sandboxed fuzzing and monitoring via Docker and eBPF. The tool detects risky capabilities through configurable rule-based analysis and provides mitigation recommendations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces mcp-sec-audit, a security assessment toolkit for MCP servers with capabilities such as static analysis and sandboxed fuzzing.
AI.motivation: The proliferation of MCP servers poses security risks due to privileged capabilities like file access and command execution.
AI.method: Static pattern matching for Python MCP servers combined with dynamic sandboxed fuzzing using Docker and eBPF technologies.
AI.result: The toolkit successfully identifies risky capabilities and offers configurable mitigation recommendations.
AI.conclusion: mcp-sec-audit enhances security for MCP servers by offering both static and dynamic analysis approaches to mitigate vulnerabilities.

title: Auction-Based Task Allocation with Energy-Conscientious Trajectory Optimization for AMR Fleets
summary: This paper presents a hierarchical two-stage framework for multi-robot task allocation and trajectory optimization in asymmetric task spaces: (1) a sequential auction allocates tasks using closed-form bid functions, and (2) each robot independently solves an optimal control problem for energy-minimal trajectories with a physics-based battery model, followed by a collision avoidance refinement step using pairwise proximity penalties. Event-triggered warm-start rescheduling with bounded trigger frequency handles robot faults, priority arrivals, and energy deviations. Across 505 scenarios with 2-20 robots and up to 100 tasks on three factory layouts, both energy- and distance-based auction variants achieve 11.8% average energy savings over nearest-task allocation, with rescheduling latency under 10 ms. The central finding is that bid-metric performance is regime-dependent: in uniform workspaces, distance bids outperform energy bids by 3.5% (p < 0.05, Wilcoxon) because a 15.7% closed-form approximation error degrades bid ranking accuracy to 87%; however, when workspace friction heterogeneity is sufficient (r < 0.85 energy-distance correlation), a zone-aware energy bid outperforms distance bids by 2-2.4%. These results provide practitioner guidance: use distance bids in near-uniform terrain and energy-aware bids when friction variation is significant.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a hierarchical two-stage framework for multi-robot task allocation and trajectory optimization, achieving notable energy savings and offering guidance on bid metric usage based on terrain characteristics.
AI.motivation: To improve task allocation and trajectory optimization in multi-robot systems in asymmetric task spaces, addressing energy efficiency, collision avoidance, and adaptability to terrain and robot-specific deviations.
AI.method: The framework consists of (1) a sequential auction with bid functions for task allocation and (2) trajectory optimization using an energy-minimal approach refined by collision avoidance. Event-triggered rescheduling adapts to dynamic conditions, such as robot faults or energy changes.
AI.result: Across 505 scenarios, the proposed methods achieve 11.8% energy savings over baseline approaches, and findings indicate that performance of bidding metrics depends on workspace characteristics (e.g., terrain uniformity).
