AI.conclusion: LLM-ODE improves upon traditional GP-based approaches and serves as a scalable and effective solution for model discovery in dynamical systems.

title: Predictive Regularization Against Visual Representation Degradation in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel at vision-language tasks, the cost of their language-driven training on internal visual foundational competence remains unclear. In this paper, we conduct a detailed diagnostic analysis to unveil a pervasive issue: visual representation degradation in MLLMs. Specifically, we find that compared to the initial visual features, the visual representation in the middle layers of LLM exhibits both a degradation in global function and patch structure. We attribute this phenomenon to a visual sacrifice driven by the singular text-generation objective, where the model compromises its visual fidelity to optimize for answer generation. We argue that a robust MLLM requires both strong cross-modal reasoning and core visual competence, and propose Predictive Regularization (PRe) to force degraded intermediate features to predict initial visual features, thereby maintaining the inherent visual attributes of the MLLM's internal representations. Extensive experiments confirm that mitigating this visual degradation effectively boosts vision-language performance, underscoring the critical importance of fostering robust internal visual representations within MLLMs for comprehensive multimodal understanding.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies visual representation degradation in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) due to language-driven training and introduces Predictive Regularization (PRe) to address this issue.
AI.motivation: To analyze and improve the visual foundational competence within MLLMs, addressing the degradation caused by text-generation objectives.
AI.method: Detailed diagnostic analysis of intermediate visual representations in MLLMs and implementation of Predictive Regularization (PRe), which forces intermediate features to predict initial visual attributes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that mitigating visual degradation with PRe improves vision-language performance.
AI.conclusion: Strengthening internal visual representations is crucial for robust multimodal understanding in MLLMs.

title: TimeTox: An LLM-Based Pipeline for Automated Extraction of Time Toxicity from Clinical Trial Protocols
summary: Time toxicity, the cumulative healthcare contact days from clinical trial participation, is an important but labor-intensive metric to extract from protocol documents. We developed TimeTox, an LLM-based pipeline for automated extraction of time toxicity from Schedule of Assessments tables. TimeTox uses Google's Gemini models in three stages: summary extraction from full-length protocol PDFs, time toxicity quantification at six cumulative timepoints for each treatment arm, and multi-run consensus via position-based arm matching. We validated against 20 synthetic schedules (240 comparisons) and assessed reproducibility on 644 real-world oncology protocols. Two architectures were compared: single-pass (vanilla) and two-stage (structure-then-count). The two-stage pipeline achieved 100% clinically acceptable accuracy ($\pm$3 days) on synthetic data (MAE 0.81 days) versus 41.5% for vanilla (MAE 9.0 days). However, on real-world protocols, the vanilla pipeline showed superior reproducibility: 95.3% clinically acceptable accuracy (IQR $\leq$ 3 days) across 3 runs on 644 protocols, with 82.0% perfect stability (IQR = 0). The production pipeline extracted time toxicity for 1,288 treatment arms across multiple disease sites. Extraction stability on real-world data, rather than accuracy on synthetic benchmarks, is the decisive factor for production LLM deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper presents TimeTox, an automated LLM-based system for extracting time toxicity from clinical trial schedules. It tested two pipeline variations and emphasized extraction stability for real data in LLM deployment.
AI.motivation: Time toxicity is a crucial metric for clinical trials but often time-consuming to extract. Automating this extraction can save effort and provide consistent results.
AI.method: TimeTox uses Google's Gemini models in a three-step pipeline: summarizing trial protocols, quantifying time toxicity for each treatment arm, and ensuring consensus through position-based alignment. Two pipeline architectures, single-pass and two-stage, were compared.
AI.result: The two-stage system achieved perfect accuracy on synthetic data, while the single-pass pipeline was more stable on real-world protocols, with 95.3% clinically acceptable accuracy and 82% perfect reproducibility across 3 runs.
AI.conclusion: Real-world extraction stability is more critical than synthetic benchmark accuracy for deploying LLMs in production. TimeTox represents a reliable approach for time toxicity extraction in clinical trials.

title: Programming Manufacturing Robots with Imperfect AI: LLMs as Tuning Experts for FDM Print Configuration Selection
summary: We use fused deposition modeling (FDM) 3D printing as a case study of how manufacturing robots can use imperfect AI to acquire process expertise. In FDM, print configuration strongly affects output quality. Yet, novice users typically rely on default configurations, trial-and-error, or recommendations from generic AI models (e.g., ChatGPT). These strategies can produce complete prints, but they do not reliably meet specific objectives. Experts iteratively tune print configurations using evidence from prior prints. We present a modular closed-loop approach that treats an LLM as a source of tuning expertise. We embed this source of expertise within a Bayesian optimization loop. An approximate evaluator scores each print configuration and returns structured diagnostics, which the LLM uses to propose natural-language adjustments that are compiled into machine-actionable guidance for optimization. On 100 Thingi10k parts, our LLM-guided loop achieves the best configuration on 78% objects with 0% likely-to-fail cases, while single-shot AI model recommendations are rarely best and exhibit 15% likely-to-fail cases. These results suggest that LLMs provide more value as constrained decision modules in evidence-driven optimization loops than as end-to-end oracles for print configuration selection. We expect this result to extend to broader LLM-based robot programming.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates how manufacturing robots can use imperfect AI, specifically Large Language Models (LLMs), in a closed-loop approach for acquiring process expertise in 3D printing.
AI.motivation: To address the failure of novice users relying on default settings or generic AI models for 3D printing configurations that fail to reliably meet specific objectives.
AI.method: They integrate an LLM into a Bayesian optimization loop, where the LLM provides iterative natural-language tuning guidance that is converted into actionable configurations for better print performance.
AI.result: On 100 3D parts, their LLM-guided system achieved optimal configurations for 78% of objects and eliminated failure cases entirely, outperforming single-shot AI model recommendations.
AI.conclusion: LLMs are superior as decision modules in evidence-driven optimization loops compared to end-to-end oracles for selecting print configurations, with potential applications in broader robotics programming.

title: Safety as Computation: Certified Answer Reuse via Capability Closure in Task-Oriented Dialogue
summary: We introduce a new paradigm for task-oriented dialogue systems: safety certification as a computational primitive for answer reuse. Current systems treat each turn independently, recomputing answers via retrieval or generation even when they are already derivable from prior state. We show that in capability-based systems, the safety certification step computes a fixed-point closure cl(At) that already contains every answer reachable from the current configuration. We operationalize this insight with a Certified Answer Store (CAS) augmented by Pre-Answer Blocks (PAB): at each certified turn, the system materializes all derivable follow-up answers together with minimal provenance witnesses. Subsequent queries are answered in sub-millisecond time via formal containment checks, eliminating redundant retrieval and generation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a paradigm for task-oriented dialogue systems to efficiently reuse answers by incorporating safety certification as a computational primitive.
AI.motivation: Current dialogue systems redundantly recompute answers for every turn, even if these answers can be derived from prior states.
AI.method: The study proposes a Certified Answer Store (CAS) equipped with Pre-Answer Blocks (PAB), ensuring that derivable follow-up answers with provenance witnesses are pre-computed and reused through formal containment checks.
AI.result: The system enables sub-millisecond query processing by eliminating unnecessary retrieval and generation of answers.
AI.conclusion: Safety certification allows for efficient and robust answer reuse in dialogue systems, improving computational efficiency.

title: Enhancing LIME using Neural Decision Trees
summary: Interpreting complex machine learning models is a critical challenge, especially for tabular data where model transparency is paramount. Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) has been a very popular framework for interpretable machine learning, also inspiring many extensions. While traditional surrogate models used in LIME variants (e.g. linear regression and decision trees) offer a degree of stability, they can struggle to faithfully capture the complex non-linear decision boundaries that are inherent in many sophisticated black-box models. This work contributes toward bridging the gap between high predictive performance and interpretable decision-making. Specifically, we propose the NDT-LIME variant that integrates Neural Decision Trees (NDTs) as surrogate models. By leveraging the structured, hierarchical nature of NDTs, our approach aims at providing more accurate and meaningful local explanations. We evaluate its effectiveness on several benchmark tabular datasets, showing consistent improvements in explanation fidelity over traditional LIME surrogates.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NDT-LIME, an improvement to LIME, using Neural Decision Trees (NDTs) as surrogate models to enhance explanation fidelity for machine learning models, especially on tabular data.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of traditional LIME surrogate models (e.g., linear regression, decision trees) in capturing complex non-linear decision boundaries of black-box machine learning models.
AI.method: The paper proposes NDT-LIME, a variant of LIME that incorporates Neural Decision Trees (NDTs) as surrogate models to provide more structured and accurate local explanations.
AI.result: NDT-LIME demonstrates improved explanation fidelity compared to traditional LIME surrogates across several benchmark tabular datasets.
AI.conclusion: NDT-LIME effectively combines interpretability and fidelity, presenting a promising approach for enhancing the explainability of black-box machine learning models for tabular data.

title: Lean Learning Beyond Clouds: Efficient Discrepancy-Conditioned Optical-SAR Fusion for Semantic Segmentation
summary: Cloud occlusion severely degrades the semantic integrity of optical remote sensing imagery. While incorporating Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) provides complementary observations, achieving efficient global modeling and reliable cross-modal fusion under cloud interference remains challenging. Existing methods rely on dense global attention to capture long-range dependencies, yet such aggregation indiscriminately propagates cloud-induced noise. Improving robustness typically entails enlarging model capacity, which further increases computational overhead. Given the large-scale and high-resolution nature of remote sensing applications, such computational demands hinder practical deployment, leading to an efficiency-reliability trade-off. To address this dilemma, we propose EDC, an efficiency-oriented and discrepancy-conditioned optical-SAR semantic segmentation framework. A tri-stream encoder with Carrier Tokens enables compact global context modeling with reduced complexity. To prevent noise contamination, we introduce a Discrepancy-Conditioned Hybrid Fusion (DCHF) mechanism that selectively suppresses unreliable regions during global aggregation. In addition, an auxiliary cloud removal branch with teacher-guided distillation enhances semantic consistency under occlusion. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EDC achieves superior accuracy and efficiency, improving mIoU by 0.56\% and 0.88\% on M3M-CR and WHU-OPT-SAR, respectively, while reducing the number of parameters by 46.7\% and accelerating inference by 1.98$\times$. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/mengcx0209/EDC.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EDC, a framework designed for efficient and robust optical-SAR semantic segmentation under cloud occlusion, demonstrating improved performance and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Cloud occlusion disrupts semantic integrity in optical remote sensing images. While SAR offers complementary insights, effectively integrating it to overcome cloud-induced noise in global modeling and fusion remains challenging without increasing computational overhead.
AI.method: The proposed EDC framework uses a tri-stream encoder with Carrier Tokens for compact global modeling and a Discrepancy-Conditioned Hybrid Fusion mechanism to selectively reduce noise. Additionally, an auxiliary cloud removal branch improves semantic consistency via teacher-guided distillation.
AI.result: EDC achieves an mIoU improvement of 0.56% on M3M-CR and 0.88% on WHU-OPT-SAR datasets. Parameter count reduces by 46.7%, and inference speed accelerates by 1.98×.
AI.conclusion: EDC successfully addresses the efficiency-reliability trade-off for optical-SAR semantic segmentation in cloud-occluded scenarios, offering better performance and reduced computational demands.

title: Beyond Memorization: Distinguishing between Reductive and Epistemic Reasoning in LLMs using Classic Logic Puzzles
summary: Epistemic reasoning requires agents to infer the state of the world from partial observations and information about other agents' knowledge. Prior work evaluating LLMs on canonical epistemic puzzles interpreted their behavior through a dichotomy between epistemic reasoning and brittle memorization. We argue that this framing is incomplete: in recent models, memorization is better understood as a special case of reduction, where a new instance is mapped onto a known problem. Instead, we introduce a reduction ladder, a sequence of modifications that progressively move instances away from a canonical epistemic puzzle, making reduction increasingly difficult while preserving the underlying logic. We find that while some large models succeed via reduction, other models fail early, and all models struggle once epistemic reasoning is required.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a nuanced framework, the 'reduction ladder,' to evaluate epistemic reasoning in LLMs. It finds models often fail when true epistemic reasoning is necessary.
AI.motivation: To address the inadequacy of the dichotomy between epistemic reasoning and brittle memorization in understanding LLMs' behavior on epistemic puzzles.
AI.method: The authors develop the 'reduction ladder,' a methodology that systematically alters instances of a canonical epistemic puzzle to assess models' reasoning capabilities and reduction limits.
AI.result: Some models succeed via reduction, but all models encounter increased difficulty when true epistemic reasoning is required, suggesting limitations.
AI.conclusion: While memorization (or reduction) explains some successes, LLMs generally struggle with authentic epistemic reasoning, revealing areas for improvement.

title: ROBOGATE: Adaptive Failure Discovery for Safe Robot Policy Deployment via Two-Stage Boundary-Focused Sampling
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures, open-source code and 30K failure pattern dataset available at https://github.com/liveplex-cpu/robogate
summary: Deploying learned robot manipulation policies in industrial settings requires rigorous pre-deployment validation, yet exhaustive testing across high-dimensional parameter spaces is intractable. We present ROBOGATE, a deployment risk management framework that combines physics-based simulation with a two-stage adaptive sampling strategy to efficiently discover failure boundaries in the operational parameter space. Stage 1 employs Latin Hypercube Sampling (LHS) across an 8-dimensional parameter space to establish a coarse failure landscape from 20,000 uniformly distributed experiments. Stage 2 applies boundary-focused sampling that concentrates 10,000 additional experiments in the 30-70% success rate transition zone, enabling precise failure boundary mapping. Using NVIDIA Isaac Sim with Newton physics, we evaluate a scripted pick-and-place controller on two robot embodiments -- Franka Panda (7-DOF) and UR5e (6-DOF) -- across 30,000 total experiments. Our logistic regression risk model achieves an AUC of 0.780 on the combined dataset (vs. 0.754 for Stage 1 alone), identifies a closed-form failure boundary equation, and reveals four universal danger zones affecting both robot platforms. We further demonstrate the framework on VLA (Vision-Language-Action) model evaluation, where Octo-Small achieves 0.0% success rate on 68 adversarial scenarios versus 100% for the scripted baseline -- a 100-point gap that underscores the challenge of deploying foundation models in industrial settings. ROBOGATE is open-source and runs on a single GPU workstation.

AI.tldr: ROBOGATE is a framework designed to efficiently identify failure boundaries for robot manipulation policies using adaptive sampling in physics-based simulation. It enables robust risk assessment for industrial deployment.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to tackle the challenge of validating robot manipulation policies across high-dimensional operational spaces where exhaustive testing is computationally infeasible.
AI.method: ROBOGATE uses a two-stage adaptive sampling process: Stage 1 involves coarse failure landscape analysis using Latin Hypercube Sampling (LHS) over 20,000 experiments, and Stage 2 focuses on precise boundary mapping in the transition zone with 10,000 additional experiments.
AI.result: The study achieved a logistic regression risk model with an AUC of 0.780 and identified four universal danger zones across two different robotic systems. It also highlighted significant challenges in deploying Vision-Language-Action models like Octo-Small in industrial contexts.
AI.conclusion: ROBOGATE demonstrates efficiency in risk management for robot deployment, providing a scalable, open-source solution for failure analysis, and revealing key insights about the operational safety of both scripted and modern AI-based controllers.

title: Beyond Correlation: Refutation-Validated Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis for Explainable Energy Market Returns
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures, submitted to Expert Systems with Applications
summary: This paper proposes a refutation-validated framework for aspect-based sentiment analysis in financial markets, addressing the limitations of correlational studies that cannot distinguish genuine associations from spurious ones. Using X data for the energy sector, we test whether aspect-level sentiment signals show robust, refutation-validated relationships with equity returns. Our pipeline combines net-ratio scoring with z-normalization, OLS with Newey West HAC errors, and refutation tests including placebo, random common cause, subset stability, and bootstrap. Across six energy tickers, only a few associations survive all checks, while renewables show aspect and horizon specific responses. While not establishing causality, the framework provides statistically robust, directionally interpretable signals, with limited sample size (six stocks, one quarter) constraining generalizability and framing this work as a methodological proof of concept.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a refutation-validated framework for aspect-based sentiment analysis in financial markets, showing statistically robust but not causal relationships between sentiment and equity returns in the energy sector.
AI.motivation: Existing correlational studies in finance often fail to differentiate between genuine and spurious associations; this paper aims to create a more robust analytical framework.
AI.method: The pipeline integrates net-ratio scoring, z-normalization, OLS with Newey West HAC errors, and a range of refutation tests like placebo and bootstrap methods, applied to equity returns of six energy companies over one quarter.
AI.result: Few sentiment-return associations passed all validation checks; aspect and time-horizon specific signals were observed, especially in renewable energy.
AI.conclusion: The framework emphasizes statistical robustness and directional interpretability but has limited generalizability due to the small sample size, serving as a methodological proof of concept.

title: Discriminative Representation Learning for Clinical Prediction
summary: Foundation models in healthcare have largely adopted self supervised pretraining objectives inherited from natural language processing and computer vision, emphasizing reconstruction and large scale representation learning prior to downstream adaptation. We revisit this paradigm in outcome centric clinical prediction settings and argue that, when high quality supervision is available, direct outcome alignment may provide a stronger inductive bias than generative pretraining. We propose a supervised deep learning framework that explicitly shapes representation geometry by maximizing inter class separation relative to within class variance, thereby concentrating model capacity along clinically meaningful axes. Across multiple longitudinal electronic health record tasks, including mortality and readmission prediction, our approach consistently outperforms masked, autoregressive, and contrastive pretraining baselines under matched model capacity. The proposed method improves discrimination, calibration, and sample efficiency, while simplifying the training pipeline to a single stage optimization. These findings suggest that in low entropy, outcome driven healthcare domains, supervision can act as the statistically optimal driver of representation learning, challenging the assumption that large scale self supervised pretraining is a prerequisite for strong clinical performance.

AI.tldr: The paper challenges the focus on self-supervised pretraining in healthcare models, proposing a supervised deep learning approach that emphasizes outcome alignment for better performance.
AI.motivation: To improve clinical prediction tasks like mortality and readmission prediction, especially when high-quality supervision is available, by overcoming limitations of generative pretraining frameworks.
AI.method: A supervised deep learning framework explicitly optimizes representation geometry to maximize inter-class separation and minimize intra-class variance, focusing on clinically meaningful axes.
AI.result: The proposed method outperformed traditional pretraining baselines in discrimination, calibration, and sample efficiency across various electronic health record tasks.
AI.conclusion: Supervised learning can be an optimal driver for representation learning in healthcare, simplifying training pipelines and questioning the necessity of large-scale self-supervised pretraining.

title: PlanaReLoc: Camera Relocalization in 3D Planar Primitives via Region-Based Structure Matching
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026. 20 pages, 15 figures. Code at https://github.com/3dv-casia/PlanaReLoc
summary: While structure-based relocalizers have long strived for point correspondences when establishing or regressing query-map associations, in this paper, we pioneer the use of planar primitives and 3D planar maps for lightweight 6-DoF camera relocalization in structured environments. Planar primitives, beyond being fundamental entities in projective geometry, also serve as region-based representations that encapsulate both structural and semantic richness. This motivates us to introduce PlanaReLoc, a streamlined plane-centric paradigm where a deep matcher associates planar primitives across the query image and the map within a learned unified embedding space, after which the 6-DoF pose is solved and refined under a robust framework. Through comprehensive experiments on the ScanNet and 12Scenes datasets across hundreds of scenes, our method demonstrates the superiority of planar primitives in facilitating reliable cross-modal structural correspondences and achieving effective camera relocalization without requiring realistically textured/colored maps, pose priors, or per-scene training. The code and data are available at https://github.com/3dv-casia/PlanaReLoc .

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PlanaReLoc, which uses planar primitives and 3D planar maps for efficient 6-DoF camera relocalization in structured environments, avoiding reliance on textured maps, pose priors, or per-scene training.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in traditional point correspondence-based relocalizers by leveraging the rich structural and semantic information of planar primitives for lightweight and reliable 6-DoF camera relocalization.
AI.method: The authors propose PlanaReLoc, a system where a deep matcher aligns planar primitives between query images and maps within a unified embedding space. A robust framework then solves and refines the camera's 6-DoF pose.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on the ScanNet and 12Scenes datasets show that PlanaReLoc excels at reliable cross-modal structural correspondences, ensuring effective relocalization without requiring textured maps or per-scene customization.
AI.conclusion: Planar primitives are effective for camera relocalization in structured settings, as demonstrated by PlanaReLoc's superior performance, robustness, and reduced dependency on map textures or prior training.

title: Benchmarking Bengali Dialectal Bias: A Multi-Stage Framework Integrating RAG-Based Translation and Human-Augmented RLAIF
summary: Large language models (LLMs) frequently exhibit performance biases against regional dialects of low-resource languages. However, frameworks to quantify these disparities remain scarce. We propose a two-phase framework to evaluate dialectal bias in LLM question-answering across nine Bengali dialects. First, we translate and gold-label standard Bengali questions into dialectal variants adopting a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline to prepare 4,000 question sets. Since traditional translation quality evaluation metrics fail on unstandardized dialects, we evaluate fidelity using an LLM-as-a-judge, which human correlation confirms outperforms legacy metrics. Second, we benchmark 19 LLMs across these gold-labeled sets, running 68,395 RLAIF evaluations validated through multi-judge agreement and human fallback. Our findings reveal severe performance drops linked to linguistic divergence. For instance, responses to the highly divergent Chittagong dialect score 5.44/10, compared to 7.68/10 for Tangail. Furthermore, increased model scale does not consistently mitigate this bias. We contribute a validated translation quality evaluation method, a rigorous benchmark dataset, and a Critical Bias Sensitivity (CBS) metric for safety-critical applications.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework to evaluate dialectal bias in language models, focusing on Bengali dialects, and finds significant performance disparities, particularly for divergent dialects.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of methods to measure and quantify biases in language model performance across regional dialects of low-resource languages, particularly Bengali.
AI.method: A two-phase framework: (1) translating and labeling questions into nine Bengali dialects using a retrieval-augmented generation pipeline and evaluating with LLM-as-a-judge; (2) benchmarking 19 LLMs with gold-labeled dialectal question sets and validating results using multiple judges and human fallback.
AI.result: The framework reveals severe performance drops in LLM answers for linguistically divergent Bengali dialects (e.g., Chittagong dialect scoring 5.44/10 vs Tangail at 7.68/10). It also shows that larger models do not consistently reduce these biases.
AI.conclusion: The study contributes a new evaluation framework, a benchmark dataset, and the Critical Bias Sensitivity metric for addressing dialectal bias in language models, highlighting the need for improved inclusivity in handling low-resource dialects.

title: Closed-Loop Verbal Reinforcement Learning for Task-Level Robotic Planning
summary: We propose a new Verbal Reinforcement Learning (VRL) framework for interpretable task-level planning in mobile robotic systems operating under execution uncertainty. The framework follows a closed-loop architecture that enables iterative policy improvement through interaction with the physical environment. In our framework, executable Behavior Trees are repeatedly refined by a Large Language Model actor using structured natural-language feedback produced by a Vision-Language Model critic that observes the physical robot and execution traces. Unlike conventional reinforcement learning, policy updates in VRL occur directly at the symbolic planning level, without gradient-based optimization. This enables transparent reasoning, explicit causal feedback, and human-interpretable policy evolution. We validate the proposed framework on a real mobile robot performing a multi-stage manipulation and navigation task under execution uncertainty. Experimental results show that the framework supports explainable policy improvements, closed-loop adaptation to execution failures, and reliable deployment on physical robotic systems.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a new Verbal Reinforcement Learning (VRL) framework for interpretable robotic task planning with iterative policy improvement through a Vision-Language Model and utilizes Behavior Trees for symbolic reasoning.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of interpretable and adaptable task planning for mobile robots under execution uncertainty, especially for systems requiring explainable decision-making and flexible adaptation.
AI.method: The framework combines Behavior Trees refined iteratively by a Large Language Model (actor) and structured natural-language feedback from a Vision-Language Model (critic), enabling symbolic, non-gradient-based policy updates.
AI.result: The VRL framework successfully enables explainable policy improvement, adapts to execution failures, and demonstrates reliable performance on a physical mobile robot performing complex tasks.
AI.conclusion: VRL offers an innovative, transparent, and interpretable approach for robotic task planning, making it suitable for real-world deployment and adaptive performance.

title: Unified-MAS: Universally Generating Domain-Specific Nodes for Empowering Automatic Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Automatic Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) generation has emerged as a promising paradigm for solving complex reasoning tasks. However, existing frameworks are fundamentally bottlenecked when applied to knowledge-intensive domains (e.g., healthcare and law). They either rely on a static library of general nodes like Chain-of-Thought, which lack specialized expertise, or attempt to generate nodes on the fly. In the latter case, the orchestrator is not only bound by its internal knowledge limits but must also simultaneously generate domain-specific logic and optimize high-level topology, leading to a severe architectural coupling that degrades overall system efficacy. To bridge this gap, we propose Unified-MAS that decouples granular node implementation from topological orchestration via offline node synthesis. Unified-MAS operates in two stages: (1) Search-Based Node Generation retrieves external open-world knowledge to synthesize specialized node blueprints, overcoming the internal knowledge limits of LLMs; and (2) Reward-Based Node Optimization utilizes a perplexity-guided reward to iteratively enhance the internal logic of bottleneck nodes. Extensive experiments across four specialized domains demonstrate that integrating Unified-MAS into four Automatic-MAS baselines yields a better performance-cost trade-off, achieving up to a 14.2% gain while significantly reducing costs. Further analysis reveals its robustness across different designer LLMs and its effectiveness on conventional tasks such as mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: Unified-MAS proposes a novel framework for multi-agent systems (MAS) focusing on overcoming limitations in knowledge-intensive domains using offline node synthesis techniques, achieving superior cost-performance efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations in existing MAS frameworks that struggle with knowledge-intensive domains, either due to static general nodes or the complexity and inefficiencies of on-the-fly node generation.
AI.method: Unified-MAS employs a two-stage approach: (1) Search-Based Node Generation that synthesizes specialized node blueprints using external knowledge, and (2) Reward-Based Node Optimization that refines logic of bottleneck nodes using perplexity-guided rewards.
AI.result: The method demonstrated up to a 14.2% performance gain compared to baselines, with reduced costs. It also showed robustness across various LLMs and effectiveness on tasks like mathematical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Unified-MAS successfully decouples node synthesis from orchestration, overcoming knowledge bottlenecks and improving efficiency and efficacy in MAS for knowledge-intensive domains.

title: Causally-Guided Diffusion for Stable Feature Selection
summary: Feature selection is fundamental to robust data-centric AI, but most existing methods optimize predictive performance under a single data distribution. This often selects spurious features that fail under distribution shifts. Motivated by principles from causal invariance, we study feature selection from a stability perspective and introduce Causally-Guided Diffusion for Stable Feature Selection (CGDFS). In CGDFS, we formalized feature selection as approximate posterior inference over feature subsets, whose posterior mass favors low prediction error and low cross-environment variance. Our framework combines three key insights: First, we formulate feature selection as stability-aware posterior sampling. Here, causal invariance serves as a soft inductive bias rather than explicit causal discovery. Second, we train a diffusion model as a learned prior over plausible continuous selection masks, combined with a stability-aware likelihood that rewards invariance across environments. This diffusion prior captures structural dependencies among features and enables scalable exploration of the combinatorially large selection space. Third, we perform guided annealed Langevin sampling that combines the diffusion prior with the stability objective, which yields a tractable, uncertainty-aware posterior inference that avoids discrete optimization and produces robust feature selections. We evaluate CGDFS on open-source real-world datasets exhibiting distribution shifts. Across both classification and regression tasks, CGDFS consistently selects more stable and transferable feature subsets, which leads to improved out-of-distribution performance and greater selection robustness compared to sparsity-based, tree-based, and stability-selection baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CGDFS, a method for stable feature selection guided by causal principles, leveraging a diffusion model and posterior inference to enhance robustness under distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Feature selection often fails under distribution shifts due to reliance on spurious features. The research seeks to improve stability and robustness using causal invariance principles.
AI.method: The method involves three main insights: using stability-aware posterior sampling guided by causal principles, employing a diffusion model as a learned prior, and combining diffusion priors with guided Langevin sampling for robust feature selection.
AI.result: Evaluated across datasets with distribution shifts, CGDFS demonstrated improved out-of-distribution performance and more stable feature selections compared to traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: CGDFS achieves superior stability and transferability in feature selection, addressing challenges related to distribution shifts in data-centric AI.

title: EruDiff: Refactoring Knowledge in Diffusion Models for Advanced Text-to-Image Synthesis
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models have achieved remarkable fidelity in synthesizing images from explicit text prompts, yet exhibit a critical deficiency in processing implicit prompts that require deep-level world knowledge, ranging from natural sciences to cultural commonsense, resulting in counter-factual synthesis. This paper traces the root of this limitation to a fundamental dislocation of the underlying knowledge structures, manifesting as a chaotic organization of implicit prompts compared to their explicit counterparts. In this paper, we propose EruDiff, which aims to refactor the knowledge within diffusion models. Specifically, we develop the Diffusion Knowledge Distribution Matching (DK-DM) to register the knowledge distribution of intractable implicit prompts with that of well-defined explicit anchors. Furthermore, to rectify the inherent biases in explicit prompt rendering, we employ the Negative-Only Reinforcement Learning (NO-RL) strategy for fine-grained correction. Rigorous empirical evaluations demonstrate that our method significantly enhances the performance of leading diffusion models, including FLUX and Qwen-Image, across both the scientific knowledge benchmark (i.e., Science-T2I) and the world knowledge benchmark (i.e., WISE), underscoring the effectiveness and generalizability. Our code is available at https://github.com/xiefan-guo/erudiff.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EruDiff to address the issue of text-to-image diffusion models failing to process implicit prompts related to complex world knowledge. It enhances knowledge structure alignment and corrects biases for improved model performance.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome the critical limitation of current diffusion models in synthesizing images from implicit text prompts that require deeper world knowledge, which are often handled poorly compared to explicit prompts.
AI.method: The paper proposes EruDiff, incorporating two techniques: (1) Diffusion Knowledge Distribution Matching (DK-DM) to align knowledge structures between implicit and explicit prompts and (2) Negative-Only Reinforcement Learning (NO-RL) for fine-grained bias correction in explicit prompt rendering.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of EruDiff in significantly improving the performance of diffusion models like FLUX and Qwen-Image on benchmarks such as Science-T2I and WISE.
AI.conclusion: EruDiff successfully addresses the knowledge dislocation issue in existing diffusion models and proves effective and generalizable, enabling enhanced synthesis performance for implicit text prompts.

title: Conspiracy Frame: a Semiotically-Driven Approach for Conspiracy Theories Detection
summary: Conspiracy theories are anti-authoritarian narratives that lead to social conflict, impacting how people perceive political information. To help in understanding this issue, we introduce the Conspiracy Frame: a fine-grained semantic representation of conspiratorial narratives derived from frame-semantics and semiotics, which spawned the Conspiracy Frames (Con.Fra.) dataset: a corpus of Telegram messages annotated at span-level. The Conspiracy Frame and Con.Fra. dataset contribute to the implementation of a more generalizable understanding and recognition of conspiracy theories. We observe the ability of LLMs to recognize this phenomenon in-domain and out-of-domain, investigating the role that frames may have in supporting this task. Results show that, while the injection of frames in an in-context approach does not lead to clear increase of performance, it has potential; the mapping of annotated spans with FrameNet shows abstract semantic patterns (e.g., `Kinship', `Ingest\_substance') that potentially pave the way for a more semantically- and semiotically-aware detection of conspiratorial narratives.

AI.tldr: The study introduces a new semantic framework, the Conspiracy Frame, and a dataset to analyze conspiratorial narratives, revealing semantic patterns with limited immediate performance gains but potential for future applications.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to better understand and identify conspiracy theories, which lead to social conflict and influence political information perception.
AI.method: Researchers developed the Conspiracy Frame based on frame-semantics and semiotics and created the Con.Fra. dataset consisting of annotated Telegram messages.
AI.result: The study observed that large language models could recognize conspiratorial narratives in various domains, and abstract semantic patterns were identified, though injecting frames did not significantly enhance performance in the short term.
AI.conclusion: The Conspiracy Frame and dataset demonstrate potential for advancing the semantic and semiotic detection of conspiratorial narratives, despite current limitations in directly improving model performance.

title: Cross-Modal Reinforcement Learning for Navigation with Degraded Depth Measurements
comment: Accepted to the 24th European Control Conference (ECC) 2026
summary: This paper presents a cross-modal learning framework that exploits complementary information from depth and grayscale images for robust navigation. We introduce a Cross-Modal Wasserstein Autoencoder that learns shared latent representations by enforcing cross-modal consistency, enabling the system to infer depth-relevant features from grayscale observations when depth measurements are corrupted. The learned representations are integrated with a Reinforcement Learning-based policy for collision-free navigation in unstructured environments when depth sensors experience degradation due to adverse conditions such as poor lighting or reflective surfaces. Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that our approach maintains robust performance under significant depth degradation and successfully transfers to real environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework integrating depth and grayscale images using a Cross-Modal Wasserstein Autoencoder for robust navigation even with corrupted depth data.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of reliable navigation in scenarios where depth sensors are compromised by adverse conditions.
AI.method: Utilizes a Cross-Modal Wasserstein Autoencoder to create shared latent representations from depth and grayscale images, combined with a Reinforcement Learning-based policy for navigation.
AI.result: Simulation and real-world tests show robust navigation performance even with significant degradation in depth data.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables reliable navigation in unstructured and adverse environments by leveraging complementary depth and grayscale information, showing strong real-world applicability.

title: Stabilizing Iterative Self-Training with Verified Reasoning via Symbolic Recursive Self-Alignment
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026 Workshop on World Models. 10 pages, 3 figures, 5 tables
summary: Recursive self-improvement--where a model iteratively trains on its own outputs--promises sustained capability growth but faces a fundamental obstacle: recursive drift. As models train on self-generated data across multiple iterations, errors in intermediate reasoning compound, leading to mode collapse and performance degradation. We propose Neuro-Symbolic Recursive Self-Alignment (NSRSA), which stabilizes iterative self-training by embedding a symbolic verification subsystem that gates training data quality at the reasoning step level. Unlike outcome-only filtering (which admits "lucky guesses" with flawed reasoning), NSRSA verifies each arithmetic operation via sympy, checks logical flow consistency across reasoning steps, and enforces domain constraints. We evaluate NSRSA on GSM8K using Qwen3-4B-Thinking across 5 self-training iterations under five conditions: no verification, outcome verification, majority voting, full NSRSA symbolic verification, and NSRSA with DPO. Our filtering analysis shows that NSRSA rejects approximately 34% of correct-answer solutions that pass outcome verification, eliminating "lucky guesses" with flawed reasoning from the training set. We further demonstrate that constructing DPO preference pairs from NSRSA verification teaches the model to distinguish sound from flawed reasoning (reward accuracy 46% to 63%). NSRSA provides an extensible framework that demonstrates how external symbolic verification can make recursive self-improvement measurable and reliable within domains where automated verification is available.

AI.tldr: Recursive self-improvement can lead to performance degradation due to recursive drift. The authors address this using NSRSA, a neuro-symbolic approach with symbolic verification to stabilize iterative self-training.
AI.motivation: Address the issue of recursive drift in self-improving systems, where errors compound as a model iteratively trains on its own outputs.
AI.method: Proposes Neuro-Symbolic Recursive Self-Alignment (NSRSA), embedding symbolic verification to check reasoning step quality and enforce domain constraints during iterative self-training.
AI.result: NSRSA filters flawed reasoning even when outcome is correct, improving sound reasoning and achieving reward accuracy from 46% to 63%.
AI.conclusion: NSRSA offers a reliable framework for recursive self-improvement by integrating external symbolic verification, ensuring sound reasoning and measurable improvements in domains supporting automated verification.

title: Beyond Expression Similarity: Contrastive Learning Recovers Functional Gene Associations from Protein Interaction Structure
summary: The Predictive Associative Memory (PAM) framework posits that useful relationships often connect items that co-occur in shared contexts rather than items that appear similar in embedding space. A contrastive MLP trained on co-occurrence annotations--Contrastive Association Learning (CAL)--has improved multi-hop passage retrieval and discovered narrative function at corpus scale in text. We test whether this principle transfers to molecular biology, where protein-protein interactions provide functional associations distinct from gene expression similarity. Four experiments across two biological domains map the operating envelope. On gene perturbation data (Replogle K562 CRISPRi, 2,285 genes), CAL trained on STRING protein interactions achieves cross-boundary AUC of 0.908 where expression similarity scores 0.518. A second gene dataset (DepMap, 17,725 genes) confirms the result after negative sampling correction, reaching cross-boundary AUC of 0.947. Two drug sensitivity experiments produce informative negatives that sharpen boundary conditions. Three cross-domain findings emerge: (1) inductive transfer succeeds in biology--a node-disjoint split with unseen genes yields AUC 0.826 (Delta +0.127)--where it fails in text (+/-0.10), suggesting physically grounded associations are more transferable than contingent co-occurrences; (2) CAL scores anti-correlate with interaction degree (Spearman r = -0.590), with gains concentrating on understudied genes with focused interaction profiles; (3) tighter association quality outperforms larger but noisier training sets, reversing the text pattern. Results are stable across training seeds (SD < 0.001) and cross-boundary threshold choices.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the Predictive Associative Memory (PAM) framework in molecular biology, using a contrastive approach to uncover associations beyond standard similarity metrics.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the PAM framework and the CAL model, successful in text-based retrieval tasks, can be effectively applied to molecular biology, specifically protein-protein interactions that differ from gene expression similarities.
AI.method: A contrastive MLP model (CAL) is trained on STRING protein interaction data to assess its predictive performance across four experiments in gene perturbation and drug sensitivity domains.
AI.result: CAL achieves superior performance (AUC: 0.908 - 0.947) in predicting associations across gene datasets compared to traditional similarity scores, with specific insights into gene interaction profiles and transferability.
AI.conclusion: Physical associations are more transferable than contingent co-occurrences, and tighter association quality outperforms larger noisier datasets. The framework is robust across thresholds and training variations.

title: MERIT: Multi-domain Efficient RAW Image Translation
summary: RAW images captured by different camera sensors exhibit substantial domain shifts due to varying spectral responses, noise characteristics, and tone behaviors, complicating their direct use in downstream computer vision tasks. Prior methods address this problem by training domain-specific RAW-to-RAW translators for each source-target pair, but such approaches do not scale to real-world scenarios involving multiple types of commercial cameras. In this work, we introduce MERIT, the first unified framework for multi-domain RAW image translation, which leverages a single model to perform translations across arbitrary camera domains. To address domain-specific noise discrepancies, we propose a sensor-aware noise modeling loss that explicitly aligns the signal-dependent noise statistics of the generated images with those of the target domain. We further enhance the generator with a conditional multi-scale large kernel attention module for improved context and sensor-aware feature modeling. To facilitate standardized evaluation, we introduce MDRAW, the first dataset tailored for multi-domain RAW image translation, comprising both paired and unpaired RAW captures from five diverse camera sensors across a wide range of scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MERIT outperforms prior models in both quality (5.56 dB improvement) and scalability (80% reduction in training iterations).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MERIT, a scalable framework for RAW image translation across diverse camera domains, leveraging advanced noise modeling and attention mechanisms. It also presents the MDRAW dataset for evaluation.
AI.motivation: RAW images from different cameras exhibit domain shifts that complicate their use in vision tasks. Prior methods require domain-specific translators, which lack scalability for numerous camera types.
AI.method: MERIT utilizes sensor-aware noise modeling and a conditional multi-scale large kernel attention module for improved cross-domain RAW image translation.
AI.result: MERIT achieves significant improvements in image quality (5.56 dB) and training scalability (80% reduction in iterations), outperforming existing models.
AI.conclusion: MERIT provides a unified solution for multi-domain RAW image translation while introducing standardized benchmarks to facilitate future research.

title: Task-Specific Efficiency Analysis: When Small Language Models Outperform Large Language Models
comment: Accepted for publication at ESANN 2025. This is a task-specific efficiency analysis comparing small language models
summary: Large Language Models achieve remarkable performance but incur substantial computational costs unsuitable for resource-constrained deployments. This paper presents the first comprehensive task-specific efficiency analysis comparing 16 language models across five diverse NLP tasks. We introduce the Performance-Efficiency Ratio (PER), a novel metric integrating accuracy, throughput, memory, and latency through geometric mean normalization. Our systematic evaluation reveals that small models (0.5--3B parameters) achieve superior PER scores across all given tasks. These findings establish quantitative foundations for deploying small models in production environments prioritizing inference efficiency over marginal accuracy gains.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates 16 language models for efficiency and performance across tasks, proposing a novel metric (PER) and finds smaller models excel in efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of deploying large language models in resource-constrained environments by analyzing task-specific efficiency.
AI.method: Created and utilized the Performance-Efficiency Ratio (PER) to compare accuracy, throughput, memory, and latency across NLP tasks for 16 models.
AI.result: Smaller models (0.5--3B parameters) outperform larger ones in task-specific efficiency according to PER.
AI.conclusion: Smaller models are optimal for production environments where efficiency in inference is prioritized over accuracy.

title: Make Tracking Easy: Neural Motion Retargeting for Humanoid Whole-body Control
summary: Humanoid robots require diverse motor skills to integrate into complex environments, but bridging the kinematic and dynamic embodiment gap from human data remains a major bottleneck. We demonstrate through Hessian analysis that traditional optimization-based retargeting is inherently non-convex and prone to local optima, leading to physical artifacts like joint jumps and self-penetration. To address this, we reformulate the targeting problem as learning data distribution rather than optimizing optimal solutions, where we propose NMR, a Neural Motion Retargeting framework that transforms static geometric mapping into a dynamics-aware learned process. We first propose Clustered-Expert Physics Refinement (CEPR), a hierarchical data pipeline that leverages VAE-based motion clustering to group heterogeneous movements into latent motifs. This strategy significantly reduces the computational overhead of massively parallel reinforcement learning experts, which project and repair noisy human demonstrations onto the robot's feasible motion manifold. The resulting high-fidelity data supervises a non-autoregressive CNN-Transformer architecture that reasons over global temporal context to suppress reconstruction noise and bypass geometric traps. Experiments on the Unitree G1 humanoid across diverse dynamic tasks (e.g., martial arts, dancing) show that NMR eliminates joint jumps and significantly reduces self-collisions compared to state-of-the-art baselines. Furthermore, NMR-generated references accelerate the convergence of downstream whole-body control policies, establishing a scalable path for bridging the human-robot embodiment gap.

AI.tldr: The paper focuses on improving humanoid robot motion retargeting through a neural framework (NMR) that reduces errors like joint jumps and self-collisions while being scalable and efficient.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of traditional optimization-based methods in humanoid robot motion retargeting, which struggle with non-convexity and local optima issues, leading to physical motion artifacts.
AI.method: The authors propose NMR, a Neural Motion Retargeting framework, which includes a hierarchical data pipeline with VAE-based motion clustering (CEPR) and a non-autoregressive CNN-Transformer architecture for dynamic motion alignment.
AI.result: NMR significantly eliminates physical issues such as joint jumps and self-collisions in humanoid robot motions and enhances the convergence rates of downstream motion control policies.
AI.conclusion: NMR provides a robust and scalable approach to close the gap between human and humanoid robot motion capabilities, allowing for smoother, artifact-free integration into dynamic tasks.

title: Counterfactual Credit Policy Optimization for Multi-Agent Collaboration
summary: Collaborative multi-agent large language models (LLMs) can solve complex reasoning tasks by decomposing roles and aggregating diverse hypotheses. Yet, reinforcement learning (RL) for such systems is often undermined by credit assignment: a shared global reward obscures individual contributions, inflating update variance and encouraging free-riding. We introduce Counterfactual Credit Policy Optimization (CCPO), a framework that assigns agent-specific learning signals by estimating each agent's marginal contribution through counterfactual trajectories. CCPO builds dynamic counterfactual baselines that simulate outcomes with an agent's contribution removed, yielding role-sensitive advantages for policy optimization. To further improve stability under heterogeneous tasks and data distributions, we propose a global-history-aware normalization scheme that calibrates advantages using global rollout statistics. We evaluate CCPO on two collaboration topologies: a sequential Think--Reason dyad and multi-agent voting. Across mathematical and logical reasoning benchmarks, CCPO mitigates free-riding and outperforms strong multi-agent RL baselines, yielding finer-grained and more effective credit assignment for collaborative LLM training. Our code is available at https://github.com/bhai114/ccpo.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Counterfactual Credit Policy Optimization (CCPO), a method designed to improve credit assignment in multi-agent systems by using counterfactual baselines to assign role-sensitive advantages, and shows its benefits for collaborative large language model (LLM) training.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent systems using large language models struggle with reinforcement learning due to poor credit assignment caused by shared global rewards, leading to high update variance and free-riding.
AI.method: The authors propose CCPO, which computes agent-specific advantages by estimating each agent's marginal contribution through counterfactual trajectories. CCPO also incorporates a normalization scheme that uses global rollout statistics to handle heterogeneous tasks and data distributions.
AI.result: The proposed CCPO framework outperforms strong multi-agent reinforcement learning baselines in mitigating free-riding and assigning finer-grained credit across collaborative mathematical and logical reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: CCPO is an effective framework for improving credit assignment in collaborative multi-agent reinforcement learning, enhancing the performance and cooperation of large language models.

title: Understanding Contextual Recall in Transformers: How Finetuning Enables In-Context Reasoning over Pretraining Knowledge
summary: Transformer-based language models excel at in-context learning (ICL), where they can adapt to new tasks based on contextual examples, without parameter updates. In a specific form of ICL, which we refer to as \textit{contextual recall}, models pretrained on open-ended text leverage pairwise examples to recall specific facts in novel prompt formats. We investigate whether contextual recall emerges from pretraining alone, what finetuning is required, and what mechanisms drive the necessary representations. For this, we introduce a controlled synthetic framework where pretraining sequences consist of subject-grammar-attribute tuples, with attribute types tied to grammar statistics. We demonstrate that while such pretraining successfully yields factual knowledge, it is insufficient for contextual recall: models fail to implicitly infer attribute types when the grammar statistics are removed in ICL prompts. However, we show that finetuning on tasks requiring implicit inference, distinct from the ICL evaluation, using a subset of subjects, triggers the emergence of contextual recall across all subjects. This transition is accompanied by the formation of low-dimensional latent encodings of the shared attribute type. For mechanistic insight, we derive a construction for an attention-only transformer that replicates the transition from factual to contextual recall, corroborated by empirical validation.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how transformer-based language models can develop contextual recall, a form of in-context learning where models adapt to examples to retrieve specific facts. The study finds that pretraining alone is insufficient; finetuning is required to enable this capability.
AI.motivation: To understand whether transformer models can inherently develop contextual recall abilities during pretraining or whether additional finetuning is necessary, and to uncover the underlying mechanisms.
AI.method: A synthetic framework of subject-grammar-attribute tuples is used during pretraining. Finetuning is then applied to tasks requiring implicit inference using a subset of subjects. The transition is analyzed both conceptually and via attention-only transformer modeling.
AI.result: Pretraining yields factual knowledge but fails to produce contextual recall when prompt grammar statistics are removed. Finetuning, however, triggers contextual recall and is associated with the emergence of low-dimensional latent encodings for shared attribute types.
AI.conclusion: Contextual recall in language models does not emerge solely from pretraining; finetuning is critical. The emergence is linked to implicit latent representations, which can be mechanistically explained using attention-based architectures.

title: Dodgersort: Uncertainty-Aware VLM-Guided Human-in-the-Loop Pairwise Ranking
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures, Pacific-Asia Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining(PAKDD2026)
summary: Pairwise comparison labeling is emerging as it yields higher inter-rater reliability than conventional classification labeling, but exhaustive comparisons require quadratic cost. We propose Dodgersort, which leverages CLIP-based hierarchical pre-ordering, a neural ranking head and probabilistic ensemble (Elo, BTL, GP), epistemic--aleatoric uncertainty decomposition, and information-theoretic pair selection. It reduces human comparisons while improving the reliability of the rankings. In visual ranking tasks in medical imaging, historical dating, and aesthetics, Dodgersort achieves a 11--16\% annotation reduction while improving inter-rater reliability. Cross-domain ablations across four datasets show that neural adaptation and ensemble uncertainty are key to this gain. In FG-NET with ground-truth ages, the framework extracts 5--20$\times$ more ranking information per comparison than baselines, yielding Pareto-optimal accuracy--efficiency trade-offs.

AI.tldr: Dodgersort improves ranking reliability and reduces human annotation effort by leveraging CLIP-based pre-ordering, neural ranking, probabilistic ensemble methods, uncertainty estimation, and intelligent pair selection.
AI.motivation: Enhance pairwise comparison labeling to achieve higher inter-rater reliability while minimizing the quadratic cost associated with exhaustive comparisons.
AI.method: Integrates CLIP-based hierarchical ordering, a neural ranking model, probabilistic ensemble methods (Elo, BTL, GP), uncertainty decomposition, and information-theoretic methods to select pairs for comparison intelligently.
AI.result: In visual ranking tasks (e.g., medical imaging, historical dating, aesthetics), Dodgersort reduces annotation requirements by 11–16% while increasing inter-rater reliability. Ablations across four datasets validate neural adaptation and ensemble uncertainty as critical factors. Extracts significantly more ranking information per comparison when benchmarked on FG-NET dataset.
AI.conclusion: Dodgersort balances accuracy and efficiency, offering a scalable solution for pairwise comparison labeling with enhanced reliability and reduced human effort.

title: Multi-Perspective LLM Annotations for Valid Analyses in Subjective Tasks
summary: Large language models are increasingly used to annotate texts, but their outputs reflect some human perspectives better than others. Existing methods for correcting LLM annotation error assume a single ground truth. However, this assumption fails in subjective tasks where disagreement across demographic groups is meaningful. Here we introduce Perspective-Driven Inference, a method that treats the distribution of annotations across groups as the quantity of interest, and estimates it using a small human annotation budget. We contribute an adaptive sampling strategy that concentrates human annotation effort on groups where LLM proxies are least accurate. We evaluate on politeness and offensiveness rating tasks, showing targeted improvements for harder-to-model demographic groups relative to uniform sampling baselines, while maintaining coverage.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a method called Perspective-Driven Inference to improve language model (LLM) annotations by handling subjective tasks where demographic perspectives matter.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of annotation errors in subjective tasks where human disagreement across demographic groups is significant and current correction methods assuming a single ground truth fail.
AI.method: Developed Perspective-Driven Inference using an adaptive sampling strategy to estimate annotation distributions across demographic groups while focusing human annotation efforts on areas where LLMs struggle most.
AI.result: Demonstrated targeted improvements in politeness and offensiveness rating tasks for hard-to-model demographic groups, outperforming uniform sampling baselines while preserving equal coverage.
AI.conclusion: Perspective-Driven Inference improves the fairness and accuracy of LLM annotations in subjective tasks by prioritizing underrepresented and challenging demographic perspectives.

title: DexDrummer: In-Hand, Contact-Rich, and Long-Horizon Dexterous Robot Drumming
summary: Performing in-hand, contact-rich, and long-horizon dexterous manipulation remains an unsolved challenge in robotics. Prior hand dexterity works have considered each of these three challenges in isolation, yet do not combine these skills into a single, complex task. To further test the capabilities of dexterity, we propose drumming as a testbed for dexterous manipulation. Drumming naturally integrates all three challenges: it involves in-hand control for stabilizing and adjusting the drumstick with the fingers, contact-rich interaction through repeated striking of the drum surface, and long-horizon coordination when switching between drums and sustaining rhythmic play. We present DexDrummer, a hierarchical object-centric bimanual drumming policy trained in simulation with sim-to-real transfer. The framework reduces the exploration difficulty of pure reinforcement learning by combining trajectory planning with residual RL corrections for fast transitions between drums. A dexterous manipulation policy handles contact-rich dynamics, guided by rewards that explicitly model both finger-stick and stick-drum interactions. In simulation, we show our policy can play two styles of music: multi-drum, bimanual songs and challenging, technical exercises that require increased dexterity. Across simulated bimanual tasks, our dexterous, reactive policy outperforms a fixed grasp policy by 1.87x across easy songs and 1.22x across hard songs F1 scores. In real-world tasks, we show song performance across a multi-drum setup. DexDrummer is able to play our training song and its extended version with an F1 score of 1.0.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "DexDrummer," a simulation-trained framework for in-hand, contact-rich, and long-horizon dexterous manipulation tasks, exemplified by robotic drumming.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to advance the field of dexterous robotics manipulation by addressing the challenges of in-hand control, contact-rich interaction, and long-horizon coordination in an integrated setting.
AI.method: The authors developed "DexDrummer," a hierarchical bimanual drumming policy trained in simulation. It incorporates trajectory planning with residual Reinforcement Learning (RL) corrections and uses a reward system focused on finger-stick and stick-drum dynamics.
AI.result: Simulation tests show the policy's performance in playing multi-drum songs and technical exercises, outperforming fixed grasp policies. Real-world trials demonstrate its ability to replicate training songs with high accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Drumming provides a valid, complex testbed for evaluating robotic dexterity, showcasing the proposed hierarchical learning framework's capability in handling intricate manipulation tasks.

title: Adaptive Robust Estimator for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Multi-agent collaboration has emerged as a powerful paradigm for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models, yet it suffers from interaction-level ambiguity that blurs generation, critique, and revision, making credit assignment across agents difficult. Moreover, policy optimization in this setting is vulnerable to heavy-tailed and noisy rewards, which can bias advantage estimation and trigger unstable or even divergent training. To address both issues, we propose a robust multi-agent reinforcement learning framework for collaborative reasoning, consisting of two components: Dual-Agent Answer-Critique-Rewrite (DACR) and an Adaptive Robust Estimator (ARE). DACR decomposes reasoning into a structured three-stage pipeline: answer, critique, and rewrite, while enabling explicit attribution of each agent's marginal contribution to its partner's performance. ARE provides robust estimation of batch experience means during multi-agent policy optimization. Across mathematical reasoning and embodied intelligence benchmarks, even under noisy rewards, our method consistently outperforms the baseline in both homogeneous and heterogeneous settings. These results indicate stronger robustness to reward noise and more stable training dynamics, effectively preventing optimization failures caused by noisy reward signals.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a new framework to address multi-agent collaboration challenges in reasoning tasks, proposing a dual-agent pipeline (DACR) and a robust estimator (ARE).
AI.motivation: To overcome ambiguity in agent collaboration (generation, critique, revision) and instability issues caused by noisy and heavy-tailed rewards in multi-agent reasoning tasks.
AI.method: A two-fold approach: (1) DACR to structure reasoning into answer, critique, and rewrite stages and enable credit assignment; (2) ARE for robust policy optimization under noisy rewards.
AI.result: The proposed framework outperformed baselines in reasoning and intelligence benchmarks, showing better robustness to noisy rewards and stable training dynamics.
AI.conclusion: This method effectively mitigates optimization failures in collaborative reasoning by addressing noise and ambiguity, demonstrating its potential for multi-agent reinforcement learning.

title: Detection of adversarial intent in Human-AI teams using LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in human-AI teams as support agents for complex tasks such as information retrieval, programming, and decision-making assistance. While these agents' autonomy and contextual knowledge enables them to be useful, it also exposes them to a broad range of attacks, including data poisoning, prompt injection, and even prompt engineering. Through these attack vectors, malicious actors can manipulate an LLM agent to provide harmful information, potentially manipulating human agents to make harmful decisions. While prior work has focused on LLMs as attack targets or adversarial actors, this paper studies their potential role as defensive supervisors within mixed human-AI teams. Using a dataset consisting of multi-party conversations and decisions for a real human-AI team over a 25 round horizon, we formulate the problem of malicious behavior detection from interaction traces. We find that LLMs are capable of identifying malicious behavior in real-time, and without task-specific information, indicating the potential for task-agnostic defense. Moreover, we find that the malicious behavior of interest is not easily identified using simple heuristics, further suggesting the introduction of LLM defenders could render human teams more robust to certain classes of attack.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the use of large language models (LLMs) as defensive supervisors to detect malicious behavior in human-AI teams.
AI.motivation: Malicious actors can exploit LLMs, compromising their effectiveness in tasks like information retrieval and decision-making. This motivates the need for LLMs to serve as defensive mechanisms rather than just being attack targets.
AI.method: The authors propose using LLMs to detect malicious behavior in multi-party conversations of human-AI teams, leveraging a dataset of 25-round interactions and analyzing their performance in real-time without task-specific tuning.
AI.result: LLMs demonstrated the ability to detect malicious behavior in real-time and in a task-agnostic manner, surpassing simple heuristic-based detection methods.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can serve as real-time defenders, improving human-AI teams' robustness against certain classes of attacks, making them valuable in a defensive capacity.

title: GOLDMARK: Governed Outcome-Linked Diagnostic Model Assessment Reference Kit
summary:
Computational biomarkers (CBs) are histopathology-derived patterns extracted from hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) whole-slide images (WSIs) using artificial intelligence (AI) to predict therapeutic response or prognosis. Recently, slide-level multiple-instance learning (MIL) with pathology foundation models (PFMs) has become the standard baseline for CB development. While these methods have improved predictive performance, computational pathology lacks standardized intermediate data formats, provenance tracking, checkpointing conventions, and reproducible evaluation metrics required for clinical-grade deployment.
  We introduce GOLDMARK (https://artificialintelligencepathology.org), a standardized benchmarking framework built on a curated TCGA cohort with clinically actionable OncoKB level 1-3 biomarker labels. GOLDMARK releases structured intermediate representations, including tile coordinate maps, per-slide feature embeddings from canonical PFMs, quality-control metadata, predefined patient-level splits, trained slide-level models, and evaluation outputs. Models are trained on TCGA and evaluated on an independent MSKCC cohort with reciprocal testing.
  Across 33 tumor-biomarker tasks, mean AUROC was 0.689 (TCGA) and 0.630 (MSKCC). Restricting to the eight highest-performing tasks yielded mean AUROCs of 0.831 and 0.801, respectively. These tasks correspond to established morphologic-genomic associations (e.g., LGG IDH1, COAD MSI/BRAF, THCA BRAF/NRAS, BLCA FGFR3, UCEC PTEN) and showed the most stable cross-site performance. Differences between canonical encoders were modest relative to task-specific variability.
  GOLDMARK establishes a shared experimental substrate for computational pathology, enabling reproducible benchmarking and direct comparison of methods across datasets and models.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces GOLDMARK, a standardized framework for benchmarking computational biomarkers in histopathology, improving reproducibility and cross-site evaluation.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of standardization in computational pathology regarding intermediate data formats, provenance tracking, checkpointing, and evaluation metrics, which are critical for clinical-grade deployment.
AI.method: Developed GOLDMARK, a benchmarking framework that provides curated datasets, standardized intermediate representations, evaluation outputs, and reproducible metrics for clinical pathology model development.
AI.result: In a study involving 33 tumor-biomarker tasks, GOLDMARK achieved mean AUROC scores of 0.689 (TCGA) and 0.630 (MSKCC), with higher performance in specific established morphologic-genomic association tasks.
AI.conclusion: GOLDMARK serves as a reproducible platform for evaluating and comparing computational pathology models, promoting standardization and clinical-grade research.

title: Efficient Fine-Tuning Methods for Portuguese Question Answering: A Comparative Study of PEFT on BERTimbau and Exploratory Evaluation of Generative LLMs
summary: Although large language models have transformed natural language processing, their computational costs create accessibility barriers for low-resource languages such as Brazilian Portuguese. This work presents a systematic evaluation of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) and quantization techniques applied to BERTimbau for Question Answering on SQuAD-BR, the Brazilian Portuguese translation of SQuAD v1. We evaluate 40 configurations combining four PEFT methods (LoRA, DoRA, QLoRA, QDoRA) across two model sizes (Base: 110M, Large: 335M parameters). Our findings reveal three critical insights: (1) LoRA achieves 95.8\% of baseline performance on BERTimbau-Large while reducing training time by 73.5\% (F1=81.32 vs 84.86); (2) higher learning rates (2e-4) substantially improve PEFT performance, with F1 gains of up to +19.71 points over standard rates; and (3) larger models show twice the quantization resilience (loss of 4.83 vs 9.56 F1 points). These results demonstrate that encoder-based models can be efficiently fine-tuned for extractive Brazilian Portuguese QA with substantially lower computational cost than large generative LLMs, promoting more sustainable approaches aligned with \textit{Green AI} principles. An exploratory evaluation of Tucano and Sabiá on the same extractive QA benchmark shows that while generative models can reach competitive F1 scores with LoRA fine-tuning, they require up to 4.2$\times$ more GPU memory and 3$\times$ more training time than BERTimbau-Base, reinforcing the efficiency advantage of smaller encoder-based architectures for this task.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates fine-tuning and quantization techniques for BERTimbau to improve cost efficiency in Brazilian Portuguese Question Answering tasks, achieving strong performance reductions in computational overhead compared to generative language models.
AI.motivation: To address the computational accessibility challenges of large language models, especially for low-resource languages like Brazilian Portuguese, by exploring more efficient alternatives in fine-tuning and quantization.
AI.method: Testing 40 configurations using PEFT methods (LoRA, DoRA, QLoRA, QDoRA) on BERTimbau models with varying sizes (Base, Large) for the SQuAD-BR dataset.
AI.result: LoRA achieved 95.8% of baseline performance while reducing training time by 73.5%; higher learning rates significantly enhanced efficiency; larger models were more resilient to quantization losses compared to smaller models.
AI.conclusion: PEFT methods allow encoder-based models to perform well in extractive QA tasks in low-resource languages, offering sustainable, cost-effective alternatives to large generative LLMs.

title: UniDex: A Robot Foundation Suite for Universal Dexterous Hand Control from Egocentric Human Videos
summary: Dexterous manipulation remains challenging due to the cost of collecting real-robot teleoperation data, the heterogeneity of hand embodiments, and the high dimensionality of control.
